However, the Acting District Judge ordered the taxation to be made under class IV, reasoning that the original action in which the decree was obtained fell under that class. The appellant appealed this order. Issues Whether the class for taxation of costs in a claim proceeding under section 241 of the Civil Procedure Code is determined by the class of the original action or by the value of the subject matter of the claim. Whether the court has the power to order the payment of costs in claim proceedings under sections 244-246 of the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, s. 214 Civil Procedure Code, s. 241 Civil Procedure Code, s. 242 Civil Procedure Code, s. 244 Civil Procedure Code, s. 246 Civil Procedure Code, s. 209 Stamp Ordinance No. 3 of 1890 Cases Referred to: Adamjee v. Cadar Lebbe - [1-c-l-r-66] Analysis Issue 1: Determination of taxation class Rule applied: Section 214 of the Civil Procedure Code and Schedule III. Court's reasoning: The court held that the scale of taxation is determined by the amount at stake between the parties. In a claim proceeding, the claimant is not a party to the original action, and the issue is limited to whether the property is liable to be seized. The court reasoned that the value of the subject matter in such proceedings is the value of the property claimed or the amount of the decree, whichever is less. Basing the taxation on the original action's class would lead to anomalous results where a claimant's costs are determined by irrelevant factors. Finding: The class of a claim proceeding is determined by the value of the property claimed or the amount of the decree, whichever is less. Issue 2: Power to award costs Rule applied: Sections 244-246 and 209 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: It was argued that sections 244-246 only mention Fiscal's fees and charges. The court clarified that while section 209 provides the general power to award costs, sections 244-246 provide the necessary additional authority to cover specific expenses related to the claim inquiry. Finding: The court possesses the power to make orders regarding the payment of costs in claim proceedings. Interaction of Issues The findings are complementary. The determination that the court has the power to award costs (Issue 2) necessitates a clear rule for how those costs are to be calculated (Issue 1). By establishing that the claim proceeding is distinct from the original action, the court justifies why the taxation class must be based on the specific value of the claim rather than the original suit. Conclusion The order of the Acting District Judge is reversed, and the taxation of costs by the Secretary of the District Court is upheld. No costs are awarded for the appeal as the Acting District Judge followed the established practice of his court.

Keywords: Fraudulent alienation of property — Want of consideration in a bond — Insolvency of grantor — Creditors' right to impeach fraudulent conveyances — Distinction between antecedent and subsequent creditors — Requirement of fraudulent intent — Roman-Dutch law principles on fraudulent deeds
Summary: Facts The plaintiff brought an action against the administrator of the estate of the late Roche Victoria to recover a sum of money based on a bond dated 25th January 1867. The plaintiff sought to have 64 shares of a ship, which were mortgaged as security for the bond, declared executable for the claim. The administrator defended the action by asserting that the bond was voluntary, lacked consideration, and was fraudulently executed to defeat creditors. Certain creditors of the deceased, including Cargill & Co., Armitage Brothers, and E. Labroy, were permitted to intervene in the proceedings to contest the plaintiff's claim on the same grounds. The evidence presented at trial indicated that the deceased was in a state of insolvency at the time the bond was executed. The alleged consideration for the bond consisted of old loans for which no documentation, witnesses, or evidence of payment existed. Furthermore, the existence of the bond was suppressed from the administrator and the deceased's legal advisors for a significant period following the death of the deceased. The District Judge found that the bond was executed without consideration and with the intent to defraud creditors. Consequently, the court declared the bond and mortgage fraudulent as against the intervening creditors, while maintaining the validity of the claim as between the plaintiff and the heirs of the deceased. The plaintiff appealed this decision. Issues Whether the bond executed by the deceased was void as a fraudulent alienation due to a lack of consideration and the insolvency of the grantor. Whether a creditor who becomes such after the date of a fraudulent alienation may impeach the transaction under the applicable law. Applicable Law Statutes: Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853, s. 58 Cases Referred to: Jenkyn v. Vaughan - [3-drew-419] Spirett v. Willows - [34-l.j.-ch.-365] Freeman v. Pope - [39-l.j.-ch.-148] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the bond Rule applied: The court applied the principles of Roman-Dutch law and the Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853, s. 58, regarding fraudulent alienations and the requirement of consideration. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence regarding the deceased's financial state and the nature of the debt. It found that the deceased was insolvent at the time of the bond's execution and that the bond was unsupported by any credible evidence of consideration. The court concluded that the bond was a fictitious debt created to place property beyond the reach of creditors. Finding: The bond was held to be without consideration and executed with the intent to defraud creditors, rendering it void as against those creditors. Issue 2: Rights of subsequent creditors Rule applied: The court applied the Roman-Dutch law principles as interpreted by Voet (Ad Pand., XLII. 8, 14), which requires both fraudulent intent and prejudice to the creditor to set aside an alienation. Court's reasoning: The court determined that while English law (as seen in Freeman v. Pope) might allow subsequent creditors to impeach a settlement regardless of intent, the law of Ceylon requires proof of an intention to defeat future creditors or that an antecedent creditor was paid off with the money of a subsequent creditor. The court found that the deceased intended to defraud both prior and future creditors by creating a secret trust. Finding: Subsequent creditors may impeach a fraudulent alienation if it is proven that the alienation was made with the intention to defeat future creditors. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent because the determination that the bond was a sham (Issue 1) provided the factual basis for establishing the fraudulent intent required to allow subsequent creditors to challenge the transaction (Issue 2). The court's conclusion that the bond was fraudulent as against creditors was reinforced by the finding that the debtor's insolvency and the fictitious nature of the debt were part of a broader scheme to defraud all creditors. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the District Court. The bond and mortgage were declared fraudulent and void as against the intervening creditors.

Keywords: Guardianship of minors under Roman-Dutch law — Court sanction for sale of minor's immovable property — Procedural requirements for minor's property sale (public auction, reserve price) — Appointment of guardian (preferential right of surviving mother) — Scope of District Court jurisdiction over minor's estates — Investment of proceeds from minor's property sale
Summary: Facts Mana Perera, the petitioner, sought to be appointed as the curatrix over the property of her infant children following the death of her husband. The petitioner and her late husband were married in community of property, leaving the widow and children as joint owners of the estate. The estate included an undivided four-fifths share of land located at a significant distance from the family home, making it difficult for the widow to manage. The petitioner intended to sell this share to specific individuals via private contract. The District Judge of Kalutara refused the application, reasoning that the Civil Procedure Code did not provide a curator ad litem with the power to sell minor property. The petitioner appealed this decision. Issues Whether the Civil Procedure Code limits the powers of guardians regarding the sale of immovable property belonging to minors. Whether a surviving mother has a preferential right to be appointed as a guardian for her children and what conditions should be imposed upon such an appointment. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Chapter XL. Courts Ordinance, Section 64. Cases Referred to: D. C, Colombo, 392 - [Vanderstraaten's Reports, p. 102] Analysis Issue 1: Powers of guardians to sell minor property Rule applied: Roman-Dutch law, as referenced in Thompson's Institutes, Vol. II, p. 60, and Section 64 of the Courts Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the Civil Procedure Code does not limit the powers of guardians as established under Roman-Dutch law. Under this law, a guardian may sell immovable property provided they obtain the leave of the Court. The Court emphasized that the District Court has jurisdiction to sanction such dispositions if they are proven to be for the benefit of the minors. However, the Court rejected the proposal for a private sale, mandating that any such sale must be conducted by public auction with a reserve price fixed by the Court to ensure the property is not sold below its value. Finding: Guardians possess the power to sell minor property with Court sanction, provided the sale is conducted via public auction with a reserve price. Issue 2: Appointment of guardian Rule applied: Roman-Dutch law, as referenced in Thompson's Institutes, Vol. II, p. 53. Court's reasoning: The Court determined that a surviving mother has a preferential right to be appointed as a guardian. However, to safeguard the interests of the children, the Court ruled that she should not act alone and that a colleague must be appointed to act alongside her. This colleague serves to ensure proper inventory and management of the children's property. Finding: The mother is entitled to be appointed guardian, provided a colleague is appointed to act with her. Interaction of Issues The Court’s findings are interdependent; the appointment of a guardian is the procedural mechanism through which the Court exercises its jurisdiction to authorize the sale of property. By requiring the appointment of a colleague, the Court creates a system of checks and balances that allows it to safely grant the power of sale, ensuring that the guardian's actions remain transparent and beneficial to the minors. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the case is remitted to the District Court to appoint the mother as guardian along with a colleague. The District Court is directed to authorize the sale of the property by public auction, provided it is satisfied that the sale is beneficial to the minors, and to issue specific instructions regarding the investment of the proceeds.

Judgment by: Lawrie, A. C. J.; Withers, J
Keywords: Illegal consideration for promissory note — Stifling of criminal prosecution — Voiding of contract due to illegal consideration — Validity of promissory note — Criminal misappropriation charge — Sufficiency of pleadings for defence
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff initiated an action against the Defendants to recover money due on a promissory note. The first Defendant had previously been charged in a Police Court with criminal misappropriation of goods entrusted to him by the Plaintiff. While the criminal case was pending, the first Defendant and his wife, the second Defendant, executed the promissory note in favor of the Plaintiff for a portion of the value of the misappropriated goods. Simultaneously, the Plaintiff provided a receipt acknowledging full settlement of the amount due in the criminal case. The Plaintiff admitted that the receipt was granted on the condition that he would not proceed further with the criminal prosecution against the first Defendant. The District Judge initially entered judgment for the Plaintiff, finding that the note was granted in consequence of the first Defendant failing to account for the goods. The Defendants appealed this decision. Issues Whether the promissory note is void due to the inclusion of an illegal consideration, specifically the agreement to stifle a criminal prosecution. Whether the failure to explicitly plead the circumstances of the illegality in the answer precludes the court from considering the defense of illegal consideration. Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Civil Procedure (Section 75) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Illegality of Consideration Rule applied: The principle that an agreement to stifle a criminal prosecution constitutes an illegal consideration, rendering a contract void. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence, including the receipt issued by the Plaintiff, which explicitly referenced the pending criminal case. The court found that the demand for the note, its execution, and the issuance of the discharge were part of a single transaction. The Plaintiff admitted that the discharge was conditional upon his agreement to cease the criminal prosecution. The court concluded that since the promise to drop the criminal charge was a part of the consideration for the note, the entire instrument was tainted by this illegality. Finding: The promissory note is void because it was supported by an illegal consideration. Issue 2: Sufficiency of Pleadings Rule applied: Section 75 of the Code of Civil Procedure, which requires that the circumstances of a defense be stated in the answer. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Defendants' answer was imperfectly pleaded regarding the defense of illegal consideration. However, the court observed that the Plaintiff did not object to the form of the answer, understood the nature of the defense, and proceeded to trial without settling issues. Consequently, the court determined that the defect in pleading did not prevent the court from addressing the substantive issue of illegality. Finding: The court exercised its discretion to consider the defense of illegal consideration despite the procedural deficiencies in the pleadings. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. By determining that the illegality of the consideration was substantive and rendered the contract void, the court prioritized the public policy against stifling criminal prosecutions over the procedural requirement that such defenses be strictly pleaded. The failure of the Plaintiff to object to the pleadings allowed the court to reach the merits of the illegality, which ultimately necessitated the dismissal of the action. Conclusion The judgment of the District Court is set aside. The Plaintiff's action is dismissed with costs.

Keywords: Extension of time for filing answer — Interpretation of procedural statutes — Judicial discretion in civil procedure — Procedural fairness in litigation — Avoidance of ex-parte decree nisi
Summary: Facts The appellant, Babahamy, failed to file her answer on the date originally fixed by the court. She was granted leave to file the answer on a subsequent date, but she was again unable to do so. The court did not proceed to hear the case ex parte or enter a decree nisi, but instead fixed the trial for a date two months later. Before the trial date, the appellant appeared and moved for leave to file her answer, providing an affidavit explaining the reasons for her previous failure. The District Judge denied the application, interpreting Section 74 of the Civil Procedure Code as permitting only a single extension of time for filing an answer. Issues Whether Section 74 of the Civil Procedure Code limits the court to granting only one extension of time for filing an answer. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 74 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of Section 74 Rule applied: Section 74 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that the District Judge construed the provision too literally. The phrase "at any subsequent time" within Section 74 indicates that the legislature intended to allow for more than one extension of time. The court further reasoned that requiring the defendant to wait for a decree nisi and then move to set it aside would be an inconvenient and inefficient procedure. Justice and convenience dictate that the defendant should be permitted to file her answer when she is prepared to do so, provided the court has not yet proceeded to judgment. Finding: The court found that Section 74 allows for multiple extensions of time for filing an answer and that the appellant should have been granted leave to file hers. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The District Court's order denying the filing of the answer is set aside, and the appellant is granted leave to file her answer.

Keywords: Sufficiency of handwriting comparison evidence — Judicial comparison of disputed documents — Forgery conviction without expert testimony — Procedural fairness and surprise defense — Criminal forgery offence — Cheating by false documents — Quashing of conviction and new trial
Summary: Facts The accused was employed as a Value-Payable Parcel Register Clerk for Messrs. Cave & Co. in Colombo. He was initially charged with criminal misappropriation of goods entrusted to him by his employer. During the inquiry, the prosecution produced two letters, marked C and D, which were purportedly written by a person named Fernando from Kandy to order goods from the company. It was subsequently established that Fernando was a fictitious person. The prosecution also produced two books, marked B and G, which contained entries admitted to be in the handwriting of the accused. The Police Magistrate framed three additional charges against the accused: forgery under Section 457 of the Penal Code, cheating under Section 403 of the Penal Code, and criminal breach of trust under Section 391 of the Penal Code. The accused consented to a summary trial before the Police Magistrate. The Magistrate compared the handwriting in the letters C and D with the entries in books B and G and concluded that the accused had authored the forged letters. Based primarily on this comparison, the Magistrate found the accused guilty of forgery and cheating and sentenced him to nine months of rigorous imprisonment. The accused appealed the conviction. Issues Whether a Judge or jury may convict an accused of forgery based solely on a comparison of handwriting between a disputed document and a genuine document without expert testimony or witness evidence. Whether the accused was unfairly prejudiced by the Magistrate’s reliance on a comparison of handwriting that was not explicitly introduced for that purpose during the trial. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code (Section 391, 403, 457) 28 Victoria, c. 18 (Section 8) Common Law Procedure Act, 1854 (Section 27) Cases Referred to: Regina v. Harvey - [11-cox-cl-546] Solita v. Yarrow - [1-moody-and-robinson-133] Griffith v. Williams - [2-crompton-and-jervis-47] Cobbett v. Kilminster - [4-foster-and-finlayson] Allport v. Meek - [4-carrington-and-payne-267] Doe dem. Mudd v. Suckermore - [5-adolphus-and-ellis-703] Doe dem. Perry v. Newton - [5-adolphus-and-ellis-516] Analysis Issue 1: Sufficiency of handwriting comparison Rule applied: Section 8 of 28 Victoria, c. 18, which permits the comparison of disputed writing with writing proved to be genuine. Court's reasoning: The Court examined whether a judicial officer could rely exclusively on their own visual comparison of documents to establish forgery. While the Court acknowledged that English law allows for such comparisons, it emphasized that relying solely on this method for criminal convictions is dangerous. The Court held that before a Judge acts on their own comparison, there must be some foundational proof or expert testimony to support the conclusion that the disputed document was written by the accused. Finding: A conviction for forgery should not rest solely on a Judge's unaided comparison of handwriting without supporting evidence from experts or witnesses familiar with the accused's handwriting. Issue 2: Procedural fairness and surprise Rule applied: Principles of natural justice regarding the right to meet a charge. Court's reasoning: The accused was originally charged with misappropriation and was not prepared to defend against a forgery charge based on handwriting analysis. Because the Magistrate conducted the comparison after the close of the case without prior notice, the accused had no opportunity to challenge the validity of the comparison or the authenticity of the books used as a standard. Finding: The accused was taken by surprise, and the conviction was procedurally unfair as he was denied the opportunity to properly contest the evidence used to convict him. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are interdependent. The lack of expert evidence (Issue 1) exacerbated the prejudice caused by the surprise nature of the Magistrate's findings (Issue 2). Because the Magistrate acted as both the trier of fact and the "expert" witness, the accused was effectively deprived of the ability to cross-examine the evidence that formed the sole basis of his conviction. Conclusion The conviction is quashed, and the case is remitted to the lower court for a new trial on the charges of forgery and cheating. The Court suggested that the new trial should be conducted with the assistance of assessors or by a District Judge.

Keywords: Owner's liability for animal-caused injuries — Roman-Dutch law actio de pauperie principles — Assessment of direct and consequential damages — Defense of contributory negligence — Distinction between actio de pauperie and lex Aquilia — Interpretation of road use regulations — Procedural fairness in issue framing
Summary: Facts The plaintiffs initiated an action to recover damages for the loss of a calf killed by a pack of hounds owned by the defendant. The plaintiffs sought compensation for the value of the calf and for the loss of milk resulting from the calf's death. The defendant admitted that his hounds killed the calf while he was exercising them on a high road but contended that he had taken all reasonable precautions to prevent the incident. The defendant further argued that the plaintiffs were contributorily negligent by allowing the calf to stray onto the public highway and that the presence of the calf on the road violated the law. The Commissioner of Requests held that the action was maintainable in law but dismissed the claim on the basis that the plaintiffs were at fault for allowing the calf to stray untended on a public highway. The plaintiffs appealed against this dismissal. Issues Whether the owner of a pack of hounds is liable in damages for the death of a calf killed by the hounds on a public highway. Whether the plaintiffs’ failure to tend the calf on the public highway constitutes contributory negligence sufficient to bar recovery. Whether the loss of milk resulting from the death of the calf is a recoverable head of damage. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 10 of 1861, Section 94 Cases Referred to: Folkard v. Anderson - [Ramanathan's Reports, 1862, p. 68] Analysis Issue 1: Liability for injury by animals Rule applied: The Roman-Dutch law principle of actio de pauperie and the lex aquilia. Court's reasoning: Under Roman-Dutch law, if an animal of gentle disposition causes damage without fault or provocation, the actio de pauperie allows the owner to either pay damages or surrender the animal. However, if the owner is at fault—such as by taking the animal where it ought not to be or failing to control it—the owner is liable under the lex aquilia for the full amount of damages. The court found that the defendant was in control of the hounds and failed to prevent them from killing the calf, thereby establishing liability. Finding: The defendant is liable for the death of the calf. Issue 2: Contributory negligence Rule applied: The principle that negligence must directly contribute to the injury. Court's reasoning: The defendant argued that the plaintiffs were negligent by allowing the calf to stray on the road. The court rejected this, noting that even if the plaintiffs were negligent, there was no evidence that the presence of an attendant would have prevented the hounds from killing the calf, given that the defendant and his brother were present and unable to stop the attack. Furthermore, the court noted that the calf was not an animal prohibited from the road under Section 94 of Ordinance No. 10 of 1861. Finding: The defense of contributory negligence fails. Issue 3: Quantum of damages Rule applied: The principle of natural and probable consequences. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the loss of milk from the mother cow was a natural and probable consequence of the calf's death. The court explicitly rejected the limitation suggested in Folkard v. Anderson that compensation should not exceed the value of the animal that caused the injury, holding that the owner must pay the full assessed damages unless they surrender the offending animal. Finding: The plaintiffs are entitled to recover both the value of the calf and the loss of milk. Interaction of Issues The court’s finding on the defendant's liability under the lex aquilia (Issue 1) rendered the limitation of the actio de pauperie irrelevant. By rejecting the contributory negligence defense (Issue 2), the court ensured that the full quantum of damages (Issue 3), including consequential losses, could be recovered without reduction. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the Commissioner is set aside. The defendant is held liable for the full amount of damages claimed by the plaintiffs.

Keywords: Intentional omission to give information of offence — Duty of police officer to report crime — Mistake as to duty — Nominal punishment for technical offence — Rape reporting obligation — Amicable settlement affecting reporting duty
Summary: Facts The appellant, a police officer, was charged with the intentional omission to give information of an offence under Section 199 of the Penal Code. The appellant had received information that a woman had been raped by her cousin, Janis. Following a complaint made to the Mudaliyar, the accused was sent to the appellant. The parties involved sought to settle the matter through marriage to avoid the public disgrace of a trial. The appellant was present during these negotiations and did not report the offence to the nearest Police Magistrate. The appellant was subsequently convicted by the Magistrate and sentenced to a fine of Rs. 50 or six weeks of rigorous imprisonment. Issues Whether the appellant’s failure to report the offence of rape to the nearest Police Magistrate constituted an offence under Section 199 of the Penal Code. Whether the appellant’s belief that he was not required to report the offence due to the parties' intent to marry constitutes a mitigating factor for sentencing. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code, s. 199 Criminal Procedure Code, s. 24 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Liability for Omission Rule applied: Section 199 of the Penal Code and Section 24 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court found that the appellant had a clear legal duty as a police officer to report offences to the nearest Police Magistrate. The evidence confirmed that the appellant received information regarding the rape and failed to report it, thereby satisfying the elements of the offence under Section 199 of the Penal Code. Finding: The appellant was guilty of the offence as charged. Issue 2: Sentencing and Mistake of Duty Rule applied: Judicial discretion in sentencing. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that the appellant acted under a mistaken belief that he was not bound to report the crime because the parties involved were attempting to settle the matter through marriage to avoid public disgrace. While the court emphasized that it would not tolerate the deliberate suppression of crime, it determined that the appellant’s actions in this specific instance were the result of a mistake regarding his official duty rather than a malicious attempt to suppress justice. Finding: The sentence was excessive for a technical offence committed under a mistake of duty; the fine was reduced to a nominal amount. Interaction of Issues The court’s finding of guilt on the primary charge necessitated a review of the sentence. The determination that the appellant acted under a "mistake as to his duty" directly influenced the court's decision to characterize the offence as "technical," thereby justifying a significant reduction in the penalty imposed by the Magistrate. Conclusion The conviction of the appellant is upheld. The sentence is modified, and the fine is reduced to Re. 1.

Parties: Le Mesurier v. Le Mesurier Et Al.
Bench: The Lord Chancellor, J.; Lord Watson, J.; Lord Hobhouse, J.; Lord Macnaghten, J.; Lord Morris, J.; R. Couch, J.
Keywords: Jurisdiction in divorce cases — Domicile as test for matrimonial jurisdiction — Applicability of Roman-Dutch law — Matrimonial domicile theory — International private law principles — Distinction between dissolution of marriage and judicial separation — Interpretation of Civil Procedure Code on matrimonial jurisdiction
Summary: Facts The appellant, a member of the Ceylon Civil Service, married the respondent in England in 1883. The couple resided in Ceylon due to the appellant's official duties, though the appellant retained his English domicile of origin throughout his residence in the island. In 1892, the appellant initiated a divorce action in the District Court of Matara, alleging adultery by his wife with three other defendants. The alleged acts of adultery occurred primarily outside of Ceylon, specifically on steamships and in Europe, with only one instance alleged to have occurred in Kandy. The respondent challenged the jurisdiction of the District Court to entertain the suit. The District Judge ruled that section 597 of the Civil Procedure Code conferred jurisdiction. The District Court subsequently found the charges proven and granted a decree nisi. Upon appeal, the Supreme Court reversed this decision and dismissed the action, holding that the courts of Ceylon lacked jurisdiction to dissolve a marriage between British or European spouses who were merely resident in the island. The appellant appealed this decision to the Privy Council. Issues Whether the District Court of Matara had jurisdiction to entertain a suit for divorce a vinculo matrimonii between spouses who were resident in Ceylon but domiciled in England. Whether section 597 of the Civil Procedure Code No. 2 of 1889 empowered the District Court to entertain a divorce suit not previously cognizable by the courts of the island. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code No. 2 of 1889 Royal Charter of 1801 Charter of Justice of 18th February 1833 Cases Referred to: Lolley's case - [2-russ-and-ry-237] Shaw v. Gould - [3-e-and-i-ap-85] Tollemache v. Tollemache - [1-s-and-t-557] Yelverton v. Yelverton - [1-s-and-t-574] Brodie v. Brodie - [2-s-and-t-259] Manning v. Manning - [2-p-and-d-223] Wilson v. Wilson - [2-p-and-d-435] Niboyet v. Niboyet - [3-p-d-1] Pitt v. Pitt - [1-sess-ca-3rd-series-106] Shields v. Shield - [4-sess-ca-2nd-series-142] Jack v. Jack - [24-sess-ca-2nd-series-467] Hume v. Hume - [24-sess-ca-2nd-series-1342] Stavert v. Stavert - [9-sess-ca-4th-series-529] Dolphin v. Robins - [7-h-l-ca-390] Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction to dissolve marriage Rule applied: The principles of International law, which dictate that the true domicile of the married pair is the only test of jurisdiction to dissolve a marriage. Court's reasoning: The court examined the doctrine of "matrimonial domicile" and found it to be imprecise and lacking in judicial authority. It concluded that while courts of residence may grant remedies such as alimony or judicial separation for misconduct, the dissolution of the marriage bond requires the court of the parties' true domicile. Since the appellant retained his English domicile of origin, the courts of Ceylon lacked the authority to dissolve the marriage. Finding: The District Court of Matara lacked jurisdiction to entertain the suit for divorce. Issue 2: Scope of Section 597 of the Civil Procedure Code Rule applied: Section 597 of the Civil Procedure Code No. 2 of 1889. Court's reasoning: The court determined that section 597 is a procedural enactment intended to distribute matrimonial jurisdiction among the various courts of the colony. It does not create new substantive jurisdiction to entertain divorce suits that were not previously cognizable by the courts of the island under the applicable law. Finding: Section 597 did not confer jurisdiction upon the District Court to hear the appellant's divorce suit. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that section 597 is merely procedural (Issue 2) confirms that the court must look to the substantive law of the island to determine if jurisdiction exists. The conclusion that the substantive law, guided by International law, limits divorce jurisdiction to the court of the parties' domicile (Issue 1) effectively precludes the District Court from hearing the case, regardless of the procedural provisions of the Code. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the order of the Supreme Court is affirmed. The appellant is ordered to pay the costs of the first and fourth respondents.

Keywords: Keeping a common gaming-place — Illegality of 'Kurunegala rouge et blanc' — Interpretation of bagatelle exemption — Wagering and staking money for profit — Elements of a common gaming house — Appellate review of conviction and sentence
Summary: Facts The two accused persons were the occupiers of a house located in a densely populated area of Kurunegala. Within this house, a bagatelle table was maintained and used for a game described as Kurunegala rouge et blanc. The house served as a nightly resort for various members of the public. During the game, a red ball was placed on the table, and a player struck it with a white ball. Spectators placed monetary stakes on which ball would fall into a hole. The accused collected a commission from these stakes before distributing the remainder to the winners. The Police Magistrate convicted the accused of keeping a common gaming-place under the Ordinance No. 17 of 1889 and imposed a fine of Rs. 250 on each. The accused appealed the conviction. Issues Whether the game of Kurunegala rouge et blanc constitutes the game of bagatelle as sanctioned under Section 18 of the Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. Whether the accused are liable for keeping a common gaming-place under Section 5 of the Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 17 of 1889, ss. 5 and 18 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Classification of the game Rule applied: Section 18 of the Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. Court's reasoning: The court examined the mechanics of the game played by the accused, noting that it involved betting on the outcome of a ball falling into a hole after a specific stroke. The court rejected the appellants' argument that this was merely a variation of bagatelle, similar to how snooker is a variation of billiards. The court affirmed the Magistrate's finding that the game played was distinct from the game of bagatelle protected by the Ordinance. Finding: The game of Kurunegala rouge et blanc is not the game of bagatelle sanctioned by the Ordinance. Issue 2: Liability for keeping a common gaming-place Rule applied: Section 5 of the Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. Court's reasoning: The court found that the accused occupied the premises, allowed public access, and facilitated a game for stakes where they personally profited by taking a commission from the bets placed. Because the game was not the protected game of bagatelle, the premises functioned as a common gaming-place. Finding: The accused are liable under Section 5 of the Ordinance for keeping a common gaming-place. Interaction of Issues The finding that the game played was not the sanctioned game of bagatelle was the prerequisite for establishing that the premises were being used as a common gaming-place. By determining that the game fell outside the protection of Section 18, the court effectively removed the legal shield the appellants sought to use to justify their activities under Section 5. Conclusion The conviction is affirmed. The sentence is reduced to a fine of Rs. 100 for each accused, with a default sentence of three months' rigorous imprisonment.

Keywords: Computation of time for appeal — Exclusion of first day in time calculation — Criminal appeal procedure — Timeliness of legal filings — Interpretation of procedural time limits
Summary: Facts The accused was convicted by the Police Magistrate of Balapitiya on the 23rd of August, 1895. The accused subsequently tendered a petition of appeal on the 2nd of September, 1895. The Police Magistrate refused to accept or forward the petition to the Supreme Court, asserting that the appeal was filed one day beyond the prescribed time limit. Counsel for the accused moved the Supreme Court for an order directing the Police Magistrate to transmit the record of the case for appeal purposes pursuant to section 409 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Issues Whether the petition of appeal tendered on the 2nd of September against a conviction dated the 23rd of August was filed within the time prescribed by law. Applicable Law Statutes: Criminal Procedure Code, s. 408 Cases Referred to: Young v. Higgon - [6 M. & W. 49] Analysis Issue 1: Computation of time for appeal Rule applied: Section 408 of the Criminal Procedure Code, which dictates that in calculating a period within which an act is required to be done, the day from or after which such period is to be commenced is excluded, and the last day of such period is included. Court's reasoning: The court applied the principle that when time is allowed to perform an act from or after a particular period, the first day must be excluded from the calculation. By excluding the day of the conviction (23rd August), the court determined that the petition tendered on the 2nd of September fell within the permissible timeframe for filing an appeal. The court relied on established practice regarding the exclusion of the initial day to ensure the accused's right to appeal was preserved. Finding: The appeal was tendered in time, and the refusal by the Police Magistrate was incorrect. Conclusion The motion is allowed, and the Police Magistrate is ordered to transmit the record of the case to the Supreme Court. The appeal is deemed to have been filed within the statutory period.

Parties: Marshall v. Gunaratne Unnanse Et Al
Keywords: Public nuisance by religious body — Beating drums and making noise at night — Interpretation of Police Ordinance licensing provisions — Disturbance of public repose — Liability of religious leader for nuisance — Distinction between Police Ordinance and Penal Code offences — Procedural irregularity in charge and conviction
Summary: Facts The appellant, the chief monk of the Buddhist Vihare at Hunupitiya, was prosecuted under section 90 of Ordinance No. 16 of 1865. The charge alleged that he beat tom-toms between 6 P.M. and 12 P.M. without a license. During the month of June, special services were held at the temple involving the continuous beating of tom-toms and tolling of bells, which caused significant noise and annoyance to the neighborhood. The Police Magistrate acquitted the second accused, who was the principal trustee, but convicted the appellant of having tom-toms beaten without a license and imposed a fine. The evidence presented at trial did not establish that the appellant was present at the services or involved in their management. Issues Whether the conviction of the appellant under section 90 of Ordinance No. 16 of 1865 was supported by the evidence. Whether a license obtained under section 90 of Ordinance No. 16 of 1865 provides immunity against proceedings for public nuisance under the Penal Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 16 of 1865, s. 90 Penal Code Cases Referred to: Holland v. Kapugama Terunnanse - [1-s-c-c-90] Jansz v. Endoris - [9-s-c-c-204] Analysis Issue 1: Sufficiency of evidence for conviction Rule applied: Section 90 of Ordinance No. 16 of 1865. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the appellant was charged with beating tom-toms but was convicted of "having tom-toms beaten." The court observed that there was no evidence presented to show that the appellant was present at the services or that he had any role in the management of the events that caused the noise. Consequently, the court found that the evidence was insufficient to sustain the conviction. Finding: The conviction was set aside due to a lack of evidence linking the appellant to the commission of the offense. Issue 2: Scope of license protection Rule applied: Section 90 of Ordinance No. 16 of 1865 and the Penal Code. Court's reasoning: The court clarified that while a license under the Police Ordinance might protect an individual from prosecution under that specific Ordinance, it does not grant a religious body the right to commit a public nuisance. The court emphasized that religious ceremonies do not provide immunity for creating noise that constitutes an annoyance to the neighborhood under the Penal Code. Finding: A license under the Police Ordinance is not a defense against proceedings for public nuisance under the Penal Code. Interaction of Issues The findings are distinct but complementary. The court first determined that the conviction was legally unsustainable due to a lack of evidence regarding the appellant's participation. It then used the opportunity to clarify the legal limits of the Police Ordinance, ensuring that the acquittal of the appellant was not interpreted as a judicial endorsement of the noise caused by the religious services. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the conviction of the appellant is set aside. The court held that there was no evidence to support the charge and clarified that religious bodies are not exempt from Penal Code provisions regarding public nuisance.

Parties: In The Matter Of Application Of John Ferguson
Bench: Morgan, J; Stewart, J; Cayley, J
Keywords: Writ of Prohibition application — Contempt of Court not committed ex facie — Jurisdiction of District Courts to punish contempt — Distinction between Superior and Inferior Courts' powers — Summary attachment for constructive contempt — Timeliness of plea to jurisdiction — Adequacy of appeal as alternative remedy
Summary: Facts John Ferguson applied for a writ of prohibition against the District Judge of Colombo. The District Judge had issued a rule nisi against one of the editors of the Observer newspaper, requiring him to show cause why he should not be attached for contempt. This contempt charge arose from the publication of a letter signed "A B C" in the newspaper, which contained allegedly false and defamatory remarks regarding the administration of justice and judicial business in the District Court of Colombo. The letter did not pertain to any specific pending cause. Upon receiving the rule nisi, the applicant appeared before the District Court and requested time to adduce evidence. Subsequently, the applicant sought a writ of prohibition from the Supreme Court to restrain the District Judge from proceeding with the contempt matter. The Supreme Court granted a rule nisi for prohibition and stayed the proceedings in the District Court. The District Judge raised preliminary objections, arguing that the applicant had waived his right to challenge jurisdiction by initially asking for time, that prohibition was not the appropriate remedy given the availability of an appeal, and that the applicant failed to demonstrate he would be without other remedies. Issues Whether the applicant was precluded from pleading to the jurisdiction of the District Court after having initially appeared and requested time to adduce evidence. Whether a writ of prohibition is a proper remedy when an appeal is available against the orders of a District Court. Whether a District Court possesses the jurisdiction to summarily punish for contempts not committed in the face of the court or by way of obstruction to its lawful orders or process. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 11 of 1868, s. 78 Regulation No. 2 of 1816 Regulation No. 15 of 1820 Cases Referred to: ex parte Fernandez - [30-l-j-exch-86] ex parte Jolliffe - [42-l-j-q-b-121] re Crawford - [3-jur-955] MacDermott - [5-moore-p-c-469] in re Shaw - [ramanathans-rep-1860-62-116] King v. Crowle - [2-burrows] Marshall - [marshall-62] D. C. Jaffna 318 - [1-lorenz-15] D. C. Colombo 12,029 - [morgan-14] Brodie's case - [2-lorenz-85] ex parte Staples - [not specified] ex parte Patterson - [not specified] Rex v. Faulkner - [2-c-m-and-r-535] Roach v. Garden - [2-atkyns-469] Rex v. Jollife - [4-term-rep-285] Rex v. Fleet - [1-b-and-ald-379] Sir Francis Burdett - [4-b-and-ald-95] Rex v. Clement - [4-b-and-ald-218] Rex v. Davidson - [4-b-and-ald-329] Rex v. Gilham - [moodey-and-malkim-165] Long Wellesley's case - [2-russ-and-myl-69] Lechmere Charlton's case - [2-mylne-and-craig-326] Analysis Issue 1: Waiver of Jurisdiction Rule applied: Practice regarding summary proceedings in Police and District Courts. Court's reasoning: The court noted that in summary proceedings where formal pleadings are absent, a party is not strictly bound by the same rules of pleading as in civil suits. The court found that the applicant’s initial request for time did not constitute a waiver of the right to challenge the court's competency, especially given the novel nature of the proceedings. Finding: The applicant was not precluded from pleading to the jurisdiction. Issue 2: Availability of Prohibition Rule applied: The nature of the writ of prohibition as a remedy ex debito justitiae. Court's reasoning: The court held that prohibition is a remedy of right that lies before or after sentence. It is not necessary for an applicant to prove they have no other remedy. Furthermore, the existence of an appellate remedy does not bar the issuance of a writ of prohibition, particularly when prohibition offers a more prompt and certain resolution than an appeal, which might not stay the execution of a sentence. Finding: The writ of prohibition is a valid remedy despite the availability of an appeal. Issue 3: Jurisdiction over Constructive Contempt Rule applied: Common law principles regarding Courts of Record and Roman-Dutch law authorities (Voet, Gaill, Van Leeuwen, Christinaeus, Damhouder). Court's reasoning: The court distinguished between "actual" contempt (committed in the face of the court or obstructing its process) and "constructive" contempt (libelous comments on the court). While District Courts are Courts of Record with inherent power to punish the former, they are not Superior Courts in the sense of the English courts at Westminster. The court reviewed Roman-Dutch authorities and concluded that summary punishment for contempts not committed in the face of the court is not recognized for inferior tribunals. Finding: District Courts lack the jurisdiction to summarily punish for contempts not committed in the face of the court or by way of obstruction to its lawful orders. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the court first established the procedural propriety of the application (Issues 1 and 2) before addressing the substantive jurisdictional limit (Issue 3). By confirming that the District Court is an inferior court with limited powers, the court justified the necessity of the writ of prohibition to prevent an excess of jurisdiction. Conclusion The rule for prohibition is made absolute. The District Judge of Colombo is directed to refrain from further proceedings in the matter of the rule nisi issued against the editor of the Observer newspaper.

Keywords: Irregular recording of guilty plea — Procedural requirements for admission of guilt — Sufficiency of criminal judgment particulars — Right of appeal in summary criminal trials — Summary criminal trial procedure — Remittal for procedural irregularities
Summary: Facts The accused was charged with continuing a kraal in the Panadure lake in a manner that impeded navigation, which was alleged to be a breach of sub-section 12 of section 94 of Ordinance No. 10 of 1861. Upon appearing to the summons, the Police Magistrate explained the particulars of the offence to the accused. The Magistrate recorded that the accused pleaded guilty, accepted this admission, and imposed a fine of Rs. 5. The accused appealed the conviction, contending that the proceedings were irregular because the Magistrate failed to record the admission in the words used by the accused and that the statement made by the accused did not actually constitute a plea of guilty. Issues Whether the failure to record the accused's admission in his own words as required by section 220 of the Criminal Procedure Code rendered the proceedings irregular. Whether the failure to specify the offence and the relevant section of the law in the judgment, as required by section 372 of the Criminal Procedure Code, constituted a fatal irregularity. Applicable Law Statutes: Criminal Procedure Code, ss. 220, 372, 403 Ordinance No. 10 of 1861, s. 94(12) Ordinance No. 22 of 1890 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Recording of admission Rule applied: Section 220 of the Criminal Procedure Code (as substituted by Ordinance No. 22 of 1890). Court's reasoning: The court held that the Magistrate failed to comply with the statutory requirement to record the admission of the accused as nearly as possible in the words used by him. By merely recording "he pleads guilty," the Magistrate deprived the court of the ability to determine if the accused's actual statement—which the accused claimed in his petition was a justification based on custom—amounted to a legal admission of guilt. Finding: The proceedings were irregular due to the failure to properly record the accused's statement. Issue 2: Specification of conviction Rule applied: Section 372 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the judgment failed to specify the exact offence of which the accused was convicted or the specific section of the law under which the conviction was entered. The court emphasized that strict adherence to the requirements of section 372 is mandatory for a valid conviction in a Police Court. Finding: The judgment was irregular for failing to conform to the requirements of section 372. Interaction of Issues The irregularities identified in the recording of the plea and the drafting of the judgment are cumulative. The failure to record the accused's actual words (Issue 1) prevented the court from verifying if a valid plea existed, while the failure to specify the offence in the judgment (Issue 2) rendered the conviction legally incomplete. Together, these procedural lapses necessitated a re-hearing to ensure the accused is tried according to law. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the conviction is set aside. The case is sent back to the lower court for a re-hearing.

Parties: In The Matter Of The Insolvency Of M. L. Marikar Abdul Azis
Bench: Bonser, J; Withers, J; Browne, J
Keywords: Applicability of Civil Procedure Code to insolvency appeals — Requirement for security for appeal costs — Interpretation of Insolvency Ordinance procedural provisions — Overruling of prior judicial decision — Distinction between civil actions and insolvency matters — Effect of established legal practice
Summary: Facts The insolvent, M. L. Marikar Abdul Azis, appealed against a decision of the District Judge of Galle, who had refused to grant him a certificate of conformity under the Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853. The appellant did not provide security for the respondent's costs of the appeal. Counsel for the respondent raised a preliminary objection, arguing that the appeal was not maintainable because the appellant failed to comply with the security requirements stipulated in section 756 of the Civil Procedure Code. The respondent relied on a previous judicial decision which held that the Civil Procedure Code applied to insolvency appeals. The appellant contended that insolvency proceedings were governed by their own special procedure under the Insolvency Ordinance and that the Civil Procedure Code did not apply to such matters. Issues Whether the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code, specifically section 756, regarding the requirement to provide security for appeal costs, apply to appeals arising from insolvency proceedings under the Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853. Whether the previous decision in the matter of the insolvency of Philippo was correctly decided. Applicable Law Statutes: Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853, s. 6 Civil Procedure Code, s. 754, s. 756 Cases Referred to: Re insolvency of Philippo - [9-s-c-c-120] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of the Civil Procedure Code to insolvency appeals Rule applied: Section 6 of the Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853 and the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the Civil Procedure Code was designed to regulate civil actions, whereas insolvency proceedings are distinct "matters" rather than "actions." The Court observed that the Code is silent regarding insolvency proceedings and does not expressly or impliedly repeal the procedure established by the Insolvency Ordinance. Furthermore, the Court noted that the long-standing practice in the jurisdiction had been to treat insolvency orders as interlocutory, for which no security for costs was required. Finding: The provisions of the Civil Procedure Code regarding security for costs do not apply to appeals from orders in insolvency cases. Issue 2: Validity of the precedent in Re insolvency of Philippo Rule applied: Section 6 of the Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the decision in Re insolvency of Philippo failed to properly consider section 6 of the Insolvency Ordinance No. 7 of 1853. That section mandates that appeals be prosecuted according to existing regulations in the absence of new rules made by the Supreme Court. The Court concluded that the previous judgment incorrectly inferred that the Civil Procedure Code must govern simply because no specific new rules were framed, ignoring the established practice that existed prior to the Code. Finding: The decision in Re insolvency of Philippo was incorrectly decided and is overruled. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; by determining that the Civil Procedure Code does not govern insolvency proceedings, the Court necessarily invalidated the legal basis for the precedent in Re insolvency of Philippo. The resolution of the first issue regarding the scope of the Code rendered the second issue a direct consequence of that interpretation. Conclusion The preliminary objection is overruled, and the appeal is held to be maintainable without the provision of security for costs. The case is to proceed to be heard on its merits.

Judgment by: Bonser, C.J.; Withers, J.; Browne, J
Bench: Bonser, J; Withers, J; Browne, J
Keywords: Execution-creditor's competency in Section 247 action — Establishing judgment-debtor's prescriptive title — Interpretation of 'possession' under Prescription Ordinance — Scope and purpose of Prescription Ordinance — Supersedure of Roman-Dutch Law by statutory prescription — Procedural irregularities in mortgage decree
Summary: Facts The plaintiff initiated an action under section 247 of the Civil Procedure Code following a claim inquiry where the court released certain immovable property from seizure under a writ of execution. The plaintiff sought to have the court declare that the property belonged to his judgment-debtor, Johannis Fernando, and that it was liable to be sold to satisfy the judgment debt. The plaintiff claimed to have purchased the land at a sale by Grain Tax Commissioners, though he never obtained a formal conveyance or certificate of sale. He subsequently purported to sell the land to Johannis Fernando, who then mortgaged it back to the plaintiff. The defendants, who were the widow and daughter of the original owner, remained in possession of the land and successfully asserted their claim during the execution proceedings. The plaintiff argued that his judgment-debtor had acquired title to the land through ten years of adverse and uninterrupted possession as per the Prescription Ordinance. Issues Whether an execution-creditor in an action under section 247 of the Civil Procedure Code can establish the title of his judgment-debtor by proving ten years of adverse and uninterrupted possession by the debtor under the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Whether the possession contemplated under section 3 of the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871 extends to the possession of a third party who is not a party to the suit. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, s. 247 Civil Procedure Code, s. 219 Civil Procedure Code, s. 645 Civil Procedure Code, s. 648 Ordinance No. 22 of 1871, s. 3 Ordinance No. 5 of 1866, s. 9 Ordinance No. 8 of 1834 Regulation No. 13 of 1822 Cases Referred to: Punchirala v. Andris Appuhami - [3-s-c-r-149] Analysis Issue 1: Establishing debtor's title via prescription Rule applied: Section 3 of the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the Prescription Ordinance was intended to protect actual possessors and serves as a shield rather than a weapon of offense. The court held that the Ordinance does not allow a plaintiff to set up a third party's title to satisfy a judgment debt. The judgment-debtor was not a party to the current litigation, and therefore, the execution-creditor could not invoke the statutory possession of the debtor to establish ownership for the purpose of a levy. Finding: An execution-creditor cannot prove the title of a judgment-debtor through the debtor's prescriptive possession in a section 247 action. Issue 2: Scope of "possession" under the Ordinance Rule applied: Section 3 of the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the "possession" required to establish a prescriptive title must be that of a party to the suit or their predecessor in title. Because the judgment-debtor was not a party to the action, his possession could not be utilized to satisfy the requirements of the Ordinance. The court emphasized that the Ordinance was designed to resolve disputes between contesting parties (plaintiff and defendant) and not to validate the claims of strangers to the litigation. Finding: The possession contemplated by the Ordinance is limited to parties to the suit or their predecessors in title, excluding third parties. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. By determining that the Prescription Ordinance is a personal shield for parties to a suit, the court effectively barred the plaintiff from using the debtor's possession as a proxy for title. This rendered the plaintiff's attempt to prove the debtor's ownership through prescription legally impossible, as the debtor was not a party to the action and the plaintiff lacked the standing to assert such a claim on the debtor's behalf. Conclusion The court dismissed the plaintiff's action with costs. The judgment-debtor's possession could not be used to establish title in a section 247 action, and the plaintiff's failure to follow proper hypothecary procedure further invalidated his claim.

Keywords: Splitting of cause of action — Joint lessors' right to sue for individual share of rent — Roman-Dutch law of joint obligations — Lease cancellation remedy for rent default — Divisible monetary obligations under Roman-Dutch Law — Procedural bar under Civil Procedure Code Section 34
Summary: Facts Buddharakita Terunnanse and another were joint lessors of certain immovable property. One of the lessors previously sued the lessee for his half share of the rent for a specific period, joining the other lessor as a defendant to show cause why judgment should not be entered for that moiety. The court granted judgment in that initial action. Subsequently, both lessors initiated a new action against the lessee to recover the remaining half share of the rent for the same period, the full rent for a subsequent period, and the cancellation of the lease due to a breach of covenant. The District Judge rejected the plaint, ruling that the action was barred by section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code and that the lessors were required to sue jointly for the entirety of the rent. Issues Whether the action was barred by the provisions of section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the splitting of a cause of action. Whether joint lessors are entitled to sue the lessee separately for their respective shares of the rent. Whether a lessor is precluded from seeking the cancellation of a lease for default of rent if they have previously sued for rent. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, s. 34 Civil Procedure Code, s. 48 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Splitting of cause of action Rule applied: Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court determined that section 34, which requires an action to include the whole claim, does not apply here because the plaintiffs did not split a single cause of action. Under the applicable Roman-Dutch law, the obligation to pay rent to joint lessors is divisible. Therefore, the first lessor was entitled to sue for his moiety separately, and the subsequent action for the remaining share and further rent did not constitute an improper splitting of the claim. Finding: The action was not barred by section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code. Issue 2: Right of joint lessors to sue separately Rule applied: Roman-Dutch law as cited from Voet, book XIX., tit. 2, section 21. Court's reasoning: The court held that Roman-Dutch law does not favor joint obligations unless expressly stipulated. Obligations are considered several by default. Consequently, each joint lessor is entitled to sue for their quota of the rent, and the lessee is liable to each for their respective share. Finding: Joint lessors are entitled to sue the lessee separately for their individual shares of the rent. Issue 3: Cancellation of lease Rule applied: General principles of contract law regarding lease covenants. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the argument that a prior action for rent precludes a later action for cancellation. A lessor is not obligated to seek cancellation immediately upon a default. They may choose to recover rent for several defaults and subsequently elect to sue for the cancellation of the lease to remove an undesirable tenant. Finding: The action for cancellation of the lease was maintainable. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the obligation to pay rent is divisible under Roman-Dutch law (Issue 2) directly supports the conclusion that there was no improper splitting of a cause of action (Issue 1). Furthermore, because the court established that the lessors were not required to sue for the entire rent collectively, the procedural objections raised by the District Judge were rendered invalid, allowing the court to affirm the right to seek both rent and cancellation. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the order rejecting the plaint is set aside. The action is deemed maintainable, and the case is remanded for further proceedings.

Parties: Pieris v. Magrida Fernando Et Al
Keywords: Keeping a brothel charge — Legal definition of brothel — Proof of prostitution purpose — Insufficient evidence for conviction — Interpretation of Ordinance No. 5 of 1889 — Elements of brothel keeping offence
Summary: Facts The first accused was charged with keeping a brothel, and the second accused was charged with knowingly permitting premises of which he was the lessee to be used as a brothel. The Magistrate convicted both accused and sentenced them to pay a fine of Rs. 200 each. Evidence presented at the trial established that a number of women occupied the premises and that men of various descriptions visited the house during both the day and night. Witnesses further testified that spirits were consumed on the premises and that fights occurred among those resorting to the place. However, the prosecution failed to provide any evidence of acts of indecency or fornication occurring on the premises. Issues Whether the premises in question constituted a brothel within the meaning of Ordinance No. 5 of 1889. Whether the convictions of the first and second accused for keeping and permitting a brothel were justified in the absence of evidence of prostitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 5 of 1889 Cases Referred to: Singleton v. Ellison - [1895/v1-q-b-607] Analysis Issue 1: Definition of a brothel Rule applied: The court applied the legal definition of a brothel as a place to which persons of both sexes have recourse for the purpose of prostitution. Court's reasoning: The court relied on the precedent set in Singleton v. Ellison, which established that the term "brothel" has a specific legal acceptation. While the court acknowledged that the premises were a nuisance to the neighborhood due to the presence of visitors, the consumption of alcohol, and frequent fighting, it noted that these factors do not satisfy the legal requirement of prostitution. Finding: The court found that the premises did not constitute a brothel as there was no evidence of acts of indecency or fornication. Issue 2: Justification of convictions Rule applied: Ordinance No. 5 of 1889. Court's reasoning: Because the prosecution failed to prove that the premises were used for the purpose of prostitution, the essential element of the offense under the Ordinance was missing. The court held that the evidence of general disorder and nuisance was insufficient to sustain a conviction for keeping or permitting a brothel. Finding: The convictions were not justified and could not be supported. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked because the definition of a brothel (Issue 1) serves as the foundational requirement for the offense charged (Issue 2). Since the court determined that the premises did not meet the legal definition of a brothel, the conviction for the offense of keeping or permitting such a place necessarily failed. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the convictions are set aside. The first and second accused are acquitted and discharged.

Keywords: Right to amend plaint — Reduction of claim amount — Stamp duty assessment on amended claim — Classification of court actions based on monetary value — Judicial discretion in allowing pleadings amendments — Procedural irregularities in District Court practice
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff, as the assignee of a lease, initiated an action against the Defendant to recover Rs. 200 in rent and Rs. 1,000 in damages for breach of covenants. Prior to trial, the Plaintiff sought to amend the plaint to abandon the claim for damages and proceed solely for the recovery of rent. The Acting District Judge initially granted leave to amend but subsequently ordered the case to be taken off the roll. Upon a later motion to finalize the amendment, the Acting District Judge rejected the amended plaint. The rejection was based on the reasoning that the reduction of the claim from Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 200 would cause procedural embarrassment to the Defendant and that the parties would be required to continue paying stamp duty at the rate applicable to the higher class of the original claim. Issues Whether a plaintiff is entitled to amend a plaint to reduce the claim amount and consequently lower the class of the action for the purposes of stamp duty. Whether the court is justified in rejecting an amended plaint on the grounds that the reduction of the claim amount would cause procedural embarrassment to the defendant or necessitate the continued use of stamps corresponding to the original, higher-valued claim. Applicable Law Statutes: Stamp Ordinance Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Right to amend and reduce stamp duty Rule applied: The Stamp Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that there is no provision in the Stamp Ordinance requiring a party to continue paying stamp duty at a higher rate once the claim has been legitimately reduced. The Court held that when a plaintiff reduces the claim amount through an amendment, the action falls into a lower class, and the stamp duty payable on subsequent proceedings must be calculated according to that lower class. Finding: The Plaintiff has the right to amend the plaint to reduce the claim, and such reduction entitles the party to use stamps appropriate to the lower class of the action. Issue 2: Rejection of amended plaint Rule applied: General principles of civil procedure regarding amendments. Court's reasoning: The Court found the Acting District Judge’s rejection of the amendment to be erroneous. The Court rejected the argument that the Defendant would be "seriously embarrassed" by having a lesser liability to answer. Furthermore, the Court characterized the practice of requiring parties to pay stamp duty at the original, higher rate after a claim reduction as clearly wrong and contrary to the law. Finding: The rejection of the amended plaint was unjustified, and the case should proceed to trial on the sole remaining issue of the liability for rent. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the Court’s determination that the amendment was legally permissible (Issue 1) directly invalidated the lower court's justification for rejecting the amendment (Issue 2). By clarifying that the Stamp Ordinance does not mandate the payment of higher-class duties after a claim reduction, the Court removed the primary procedural obstacle cited by the Acting District Judge, thereby necessitating the reversal of the rejection order. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the order of the Acting District Judge rejecting the amended plaint is set aside. The case is to proceed to trial on the issue of the Defendant's liability for the Rs. 200 rent claim, with no costs allowed for proceedings subsequent to the date the amendment was originally granted, except for the costs of this appeal.

Keywords: Misjoinder of accused in criminal trials — Distinct offences of unlawful gaming and keeping a common gaming-house — Elements of unlawful gaming — Proof of "common gaming-place" as essential element — Burden of proof on prosecution to establish public access — Definition of a "common gaming-house" and "public access" — Quashing of conviction due to lack of evidence and procedural irregularity
Summary: Facts The appellants were convicted in the Police Court of Tangalla for offences under the Gaming Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. The fourteenth accused was charged with keeping a common gaming-house, while the other accused were charged with unlawful gaming. The proceedings were conducted as a joint trial for all accused. Upon appeal, it was contended that the joint trial constituted a misjoinder of charges and that the evidence presented was insufficient to sustain the convictions. The court examined whether the private residence of the fourteenth accused functioned as a common gaming-place, noting that while card games for money were occurring, there was no evidence that the house was accessible to the public. Issues Whether the joint trial of the accused for the offences of keeping a common gaming-house and unlawful gaming constituted a misjoinder under Section 207 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Whether the prosecution sufficiently proved that the premises in question constituted a common gaming-house under Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. Applicable Law Statutes: Criminal Procedure Code, Section 207 Ordinance No. 17 of 1889 Cases Referred to: [pc-matara-23756] Analysis Issue 1: Misjoinder of accused Rule applied: Section 207 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the offence of keeping a common gaming-house is distinct from the offence of unlawful gaming. Consequently, trying the fourteenth accused, who was charged with keeping the house, alongside the other accused, who were charged with unlawful gaming, violated the procedural requirements for separate trials. Finding: The joint trial was a misjoinder, and the conviction against the fourteenth accused was unsustainable on this procedural ground. Issue 2: Proof of common gaming-house Rule applied: Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. Court's reasoning: The court held that the essence of the offence under the Ordinance is the publicity of the gaming, which creates a public nuisance. While the Magistrate correctly found that card games for money were occurring, the prosecution failed to provide evidence that the house was open to the public. The court emphasized that friends or acquaintances playing for money in a private home does not constitute an offence; the prosecution must prove that the public had access to the house for gaming purposes. Finding: The prosecution failed to prove the essential element of publicity, rendering the convictions against all appellants unsustainable. Interaction of Issues The procedural error of misjoinder necessitated the quashing of the conviction against the fourteenth accused. However, the court's substantive finding that the prosecution failed to prove the essential elements of the offence under the Ordinance applied to all appellants, leading to the acquittal of the entire group regardless of the procedural irregularity. Conclusion The court set aside the convictions of all accused. All appellants were acquitted and discharged.

Keywords: Lessee's right to possessory action against lessor — Notarial lease as pro tanto alienation of property — Wrongful ejectment of lessee — Roman-Dutch law principles on leasehold tenure — Remedies for unlawful eviction and restoration of possession — Procedural efficiency and unnecessary court motions
Summary: Facts The plaintiff, a lessee of immovable property, initiated an action against his two co-lessors and a third party who had obtained a subsequent lease of the same property. The plaintiff held a notarial lease for a term of five years, for which he had paid the rent in advance and entered into occupation. After occupying the premises for over a year, the plaintiff was forcibly ejected by the defendants. The plaintiff sought restoration of possession and damages. The Acting District Judge of Colombo rejected the plaint, ruling that a tenant is barred by law from suing a landlord in ejectment and that the plaintiff's only available remedy was an action for damages for breach of covenant. The plaintiff appealed this rejection. Issues Whether a lessee under a notarial instrument is entitled to maintain an action for ejectment against a lessor or a third party. Whether the procedural motions for the issuance of summons and the forwarding of a petition of appeal are necessary under the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 7 of 1840 Ordinance No. 8 of 1863 Civil Procedure Code (Section 55, Section 91) Cases Referred to: D. C. Colombo 55,552 - [vanderstraaten-283] Perera v. Sobana - [6-scc-61] Analysis Issue 1: Right to action for ejectment Rule applied: The court applied the Roman-Dutch Law principles regarding leases, as modified by the legislative framework of the Island, specifically Ordinance No. 7 of 1840 and Ordinance No. 8 of 1863. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the lower court's reliance on Roman Law, which treated a tenant as a mere colonus or inquilinus without possessory rights. The court reasoned that modern notarial leases, which require registration, function as a pro tanto alienation. Consequently, a lessee possesses the legal remedies of an owner and possessor during the term of the lease. The court held that the plaintiff, holding a registered notarial lease, is entitled to assert his right to possession against any party, including the landlord, who ejects him. Finding: The plaint disclosed a valid cause of action, and the rejection of the plaint by the District Court was incorrect. Issue 2: Necessity of procedural motions Rule applied: Civil Procedure Code (Section 55 and Section 91). Court's reasoning: The court observed that the plaintiff's proctor filed unnecessary motions for the issuance of summons and for the forwarding of the petition of appeal. Under Section 55 of the Civil Procedure Code, it is the mandatory duty of the court to issue summons upon the acceptance of a plaint. Furthermore, the court noted that forwarding an appeal is a standard procedural step that does not require a formal motion. Section 91 of the Code restricts motions to matters incidental to an action, rather than steps in the regular course of procedure. Finding: These motions were unnecessary, served no legal purpose, and increased costs improperly. Interaction of Issues The findings on the substantive right to sue and the procedural requirements are linked by the court's emphasis on efficient judicial administration. By clarifying that a lessee has the status of a possessor, the court affirmed the legitimacy of the action, while simultaneously admonishing the parties for encumbering the record with redundant motions that deviate from the streamlined procedures established by the Civil Procedure Code. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the order of the District Court rejecting the plaint is set aside. The case is remanded for further proceedings, with the direction that the costs of the unnecessary procedural motions shall not be allowed in taxation.

Keywords: Contempt of Court for Disobedience of Order — Procedural Irregularities in Judicial Proceedings — Magistrate's Functus Officio doctrine — Ultra Vires actions by Magistrate — Conviction on Previous Admission After Discharge — Alteration and Integrity of Court Records — Revisionary Jurisdiction Over Irregular Judgments
Summary: Facts Sinnatangam filed a complaint against the petitioner and his daughter in the Police Court of Jaffna alleging criminal trespass, voluntarily causing hurt, and theft. On 17th August 1895, the parties appeared before the Magistrate. The Magistrate recorded an undertaking by the complainant to pay money to the accused and an undertaking by the accused to quit the land within three weeks. The Magistrate subsequently ordered the case withdrawn and discharged the accused. On 21st September 1895, the Magistrate issued a notice to the accused to show cause why they should not be fined for failing to obey the court's order and for an admission made on 17th August. The second accused stated he had been deceived and refused to quit the land. The Magistrate found the second accused guilty of contempt of court and also convicted him of an offence under section 314 of the Ceylon Penal Code based on his previous admission. The petitioner sought a revision of this order in the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the conviction for contempt of court was valid in the absence of a formal order of court.
