Whether the Magistrate was empowered to convict the accused on a charge from which he had been previously discharged. Whether the alterations made to the court record were procedurally regular. Applicable Law Statutes: Ceylon Penal Code, Section 314 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Contempt of Court Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a person cannot be held in contempt for disobeying an order that was not formally drawn up by the court. Court's reasoning: The court observed that no formal order existed which the petitioner could have disobeyed. Consequently, the charge of contempt of court could not be sustained. Finding: The conviction for contempt of court was set aside. Issue 2: Conviction after discharge Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a Magistrate becomes functus officio regarding a proceeding once the accused has been discharged. Court's reasoning: The Magistrate had previously discharged the accused on 17th August. The court held that it was not competent for the Magistrate to revive the matter and convict the accused on the basis of an admission made in a prior proceeding from which he had already been discharged. Finding: The conviction under section 314 of the Ceylon Penal Code was invalid. Issue 3: Alterations to the record Rule applied: The court applied the procedural requirement that any alteration or addition to a judicial record must be noted in the margin with the date and the initials of the judge. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the record contained suspicious interpolations in different colored ink that were not present during the initial review. The court emphasized that such unauthorized alterations are highly irregular. Finding: The Magistrate's actions regarding the record were found to be irregular. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the procedural irregularity of the record-keeping (Issue 3) undermined the validity of the Magistrate's reliance on alleged admissions (Issue 2), while the absence of a formal order (Issue 1) rendered the contempt conviction legally impossible. Together, these errors demonstrated that the entire proceedings were ultra vires. Conclusion The judgment and proceedings of the Police Court of Jaffna in case number 15,535 are quashed. The conviction and sentence imposed upon the petitioner are set aside.

Keywords: Possession of false weights — Requirement of fraudulent intention — Evidentiary burden for fraudulent use — Accuracy of weighing instrument testing — Presumption of fraudulent intent
Summary: Facts The appellant, a boutique-keeper, was charged under section 259 of the Penal Code as amended by Ordinance No. 11 of 1887 for being in possession of a false weight with the intention of using it fraudulently. A police sergeant ordered the appellant to bring his weights to a local boutique to be tested against standard weights. The appellant complied and produced his weights, declaring them to be all that he possessed. The sergeant alleged that a 1-lb. weight and a 1/2-lb. weight were false after testing them on a balance in another boutique. The Magistrate convicted the appellant and imposed a fine of Rs. 25. The appellant appealed the conviction, arguing that there was insufficient evidence to prove the weights were false and that there was no evidence of a fraudulent intention. Issues Whether the possession of a weight that does not conform to the standard constitutes an offence under section 259 of the Penal Code without proof of fraudulent intent. Whether the evidence presented regarding the testing of the weights was sufficient to establish that the weights were false. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code, s. 259 Ordinance No. 11 of 1887 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Requirement of fraudulent intent Rule applied: Section 259 of the Penal Code as amended by Ordinance No. 11 of 1887. Court's reasoning: The court held that the mere possession of a weight that does not conform to the standard, or even the use of such a weight, does not constitute an offence under the statute. The prosecution must prove that the possession was accompanied by an intention to use the weight fraudulently. The court noted that the appellant's conduct in voluntarily bringing his weights to be tested suggested a lack of awareness regarding any potential falsity and a lack of fraudulent intent. Finding: The conviction cannot be sustained because the prosecution failed to provide evidence of a fraudulent intention. Issue 2: Sufficiency of evidence regarding false weights Rule applied: General principles of evidence regarding the testing of weights. Court's reasoning: The court found the evidence regarding the falsity of the weights unreliable. The sergeant failed to establish that the balance used for the initial test was accurate, and the balance itself was not produced in court. Furthermore, the court expressed skepticism regarding the accuracy of the balance used in the Magistrate’s court, noting that if the weights were both significantly under and over the standard, it would be illogical for a tradesman to use them, as the gains and losses would cancel each other out. Finding: The court was not satisfied that the 1-lb. weight was proven to be false. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The failure to prove the weights were actually false (Issue 2) undermines the basis for inferring any intent, while the absence of evidence regarding fraudulent intent (Issue 1) renders the technical dispute over the weight's accuracy secondary to the lack of criminal culpability. Conclusion The conviction is set aside and the accused is acquitted. The appeal is allowed.

Judgment by: Withers, J.; Browne, A. P. J
Bench: Withers, J; Browne, A. P. J
Keywords: Defamation claim — Words per se contumelious — Presumption of malicious intent (animus injuriandi) — Rebuttal of malicious intent — Defense of privileged communication — Burden of proof on defendant for innocent motive — Sufficiency of a plea of privileged occasion
Summary: Facts The defendant presented a petition to the Secretary of the Municipal Council of Kandy. The petition contained a statement alleging that the plaintiff and certain others were secretly selling opium and bhang with the intention of making money fraudulently against the Government. The plaintiff initiated an action for defamation in the District Court of Kandy. The defendant denied the defamation and pleaded that the petition was a privileged communication made in good faith regarding a matter of interest to the Municipal Council. The District Judge rejected the defendant's pleas and awarded damages to the plaintiff. The defendant appealed against this judgment. Issues Whether the words used in the petition were per se contumelious, thereby creating a presumption of malicious intent. Whether the defendant successfully rebutted the presumption of animus injuriandi by establishing that the communication was privileged and made in good faith. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Contumelious nature of the words Rule applied: The court applied the principle from Voet (XLVII. 10, 20) stating that if words are per se contumelious, an intention to injure (animus injuriandi) is presumed, and the burden of proof shifts to the defendant to show the absence of such intent. Court's reasoning: The court examined the specific language used in the petition, which accused the plaintiff of illegal activities and fraudulent conduct against the Government. The court determined that such language is inherently contumelious. Consequently, the law presumes that the publication was made with a contumacious intent unless the defendant can prove otherwise. Finding: The words were held to be per se contumelious, and the burden of proof rested on the defendant to rebut the presumption of malice. Issue 2: Rebuttal of animus injuriandi Rule applied: The court referenced the requirements of bona fides and the necessity of a proper occasion for a communication to be considered privileged, as well as the principle from Voet (XLVII. 10, 10) regarding malicious defamation. Court's reasoning: The defendant failed to provide evidence to support his plea of privilege or to demonstrate that he acted with honesty of purpose. The court noted that the Municipal Council lacked the coercive power to address the alleged illegal acts, suggesting the defendant's appeal to the Council was not a proper occasion for such a complaint. Furthermore, the plaintiff testified under oath that he had not sold opium, which remained uncontradicted by any evidence of bona fides from the defendant. The court concluded that the defendant’s actions appeared intended to harm a rival rather than to seek legitimate redress. Finding: The defendant failed to rebut the presumption of animus injuriandi, and the plea of privilege was rejected. Interaction of Issues The finding that the words were per se contumelious established a legal presumption of malice, which placed a heavy evidentiary burden on the defendant. Because the defendant failed to provide any evidence to support his plea of privilege or to demonstrate good faith, he could not overcome the initial presumption of malice established by the nature of the language used. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the decree of the District Court is affirmed. The defendant is ordered to pay the costs of the appeal.

Parties: Nicholas De Silva v. Shaik Ali
Judgment by: Bonser, C.J.; Withers, J.; Browne, J
Bench: Bonser, J; Withers, J; Browne, J
Keywords: Rei vindicatio action — Alienation of community property by wife — Exceptio rei venditae et traditae defense — Jus retentionis for useful improvements (impensae utiles) — Mortgage payment as utilis impensa — Validity of non-registered Christian marriage — Estoppel by husband's acquiescence — Transferability of jus retentionis
Summary: Facts The plaintiff sought to vindicate immovable property originally owned by one Rodrigo, who died intestate in 1838, leaving a daughter, Johanna. Johanna married Don Juan in 1850. The couple later separated by mutual consent, and Johanna lived with one Carolis Silva. Following the death of her mother, Christina, in 1888, Johanna and Carolis Silva sold the property to Celestina Hamine to discharge a mortgage debt. Celestina Hamine subsequently sold the property to Ahamat, who then sold it to the defendant. The plaintiff, claiming as the heir and administrator of Johanna and as the purchaser of Don Juan’s interest, initiated this action for a declaration of title and ejectment. The defendant argued that the marriage between Johanna and Don Juan was not valid, that the sale was authorized, and that he was entitled to a right of retention until compensated for the mortgage debt and improvements made by his predecessors in title. The District Court found the marriage valid, rejected the defendant's claim for retention, and entered judgment for the plaintiff. Issues Whether the marriage between Johanna and Don Juan was valid under Ordinance No. 6 of 1847. Whether the sale of the property by Johanna, without the consent of her husband, was valid. Whether the defendant is entitled to the exceptio rei venditae et traditae to defeat the plaintiff's claim to the moiety inherited from Johanna. Whether the defendant is entitled to a jus retentionis for impensae utiles (useful improvements) and the mortgage debt paid by his predecessors. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 6 of 1847 Ordinance No. 13 of 1863 Regulation No. 9 of 1822 Cases Referred to: [3-s-c-c-31] [1-lor-128] [3-lor-235] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Marriage Rule applied: Ordinance No. 6 of 1847, Section 4. Court's reasoning: The court found that the marriage was solemnized by a minister of the Christian religion. Under Section 4 of Ordinance No. 6 of 1847, such a marriage does not become null and void for want of registration. The court held that the evidence sufficiently established the solemnization of the marriage. Finding: The marriage between Johanna and Don Juan was valid. Issue 2: Validity of Alienation Rule applied: Roman-Dutch law principles regarding the marital community. Court's reasoning: The court held that a wife living apart from her husband by mutual consent cannot validly alienate property belonging to the marriage community without the husband's authority or consent. There was no evidence that the husband consented to or knew of the sale. Finding: The alienation by Johanna was invalid. Issue 3: Exceptio rei venditae et traditae Rule applied: Voet, XXI. 3, 3 and XXI. 3, 4. Court's reasoning: Although the sale by Johanna was invalid, the court held that as the plaintiff claims the moiety as Johanna's heir, he is bound by her acts. It would be inequitable for the heir to repudiate the sale made by his predecessor. The defendant, as a successor in title, may successfully plead the exceptio rei venditae et traditae against the heir. Finding: The plaintiff is estopped from claiming the moiety derived from Johanna. Issue 4: Jus Retentionis Rule applied: Voet, XVI. 2, 20; Dig. X. 3, 14. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the mortgage debt paid off by the purchaser constitutes impensae utiles. A bona fide possessor who makes improvements is entitled to a jus retentionis. This right passes to subsequent purchasers without the need for a special cession. The court set out specific rules for calculating the recovery of impensae utiles, including accounting for mesne profits. Finding: The defendant is entitled to a jus retentionis until the impensae utiles are compensated. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent: the invalidity of the sale (Issue 2) necessitated the application of the exceptio rei venditae et traditae (Issue 3) to prevent the heir from unjustly enriching himself by repudiating his predecessor's act. Furthermore, the recognition of the jus retentionis (Issue 4) serves as an equitable balance, ensuring that while the plaintiff may recover the property, he must compensate the defendant for the value added to the estate. Conclusion The appeal is allowed in part. The decree is varied to grant the plaintiff recovery of only one undivided moiety of the property, subject to the defendant's right of retention until the plaintiff pays the amount determined to be due for impensae utiles. The case is remitted to the District Court for the assessment of these expenses.

Keywords: Annulment of insolvency adjudication — Technical objections in insolvency proceedings — Duty of District Court to forward appeals — Sufficiency of petitioning creditor's debt proof — Description of insolvent in petition — Partnership debt and petitioning creditor — Affidavit requirements in insolvency
Summary: Facts The appellant, a creditor of Hayman Thornhill, sought to annul an adjudication of insolvency granted by the District Court of Colombo. The insolvency petition was filed by William Jenkins, a partner of the firm Cargill & Co., regarding a debt owed to the firm. The appellant challenged the adjudication on several grounds, including the failure of all partners to sign the petition, the absence of a power of attorney authorizing the petitioner to act for the firm, and the failure of the supporting affidavit to comply with section 438 of the Civil Procedure Code. The District Court refused the application to annul the adjudication. Following the perfection of the appeal, the District Court delayed the transmission of the record to the Supreme Court at the request of the insolvent's proctor. Issues Whether the District Court has the authority to delay the forwarding of a perfected appeal to the Supreme Court. Whether a petitioning creditor's debt must be proven in accordance with section 438 of the Civil Procedure Code or the Insolvency Ordinance. Whether a petition for insolvency filed by one partner on behalf of a firm is invalid for lack of a power of attorney or the failure of all partners to sign. Whether the description of an insolvent in a petition must include more than just a name and city. Applicable Law Statutes: Insolvency Ordinance Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Delay in forwarding appeal Rule applied: The court applied the procedural requirement that a District Court must transmit a case to the Supreme Court once an appeal is perfected. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the District Court improperly allowed a motion to delay the record transmission. The court emphasized that a District Court has no legal right to delay the forwarding of an appeal once it has been perfected. Finding: The delay was irregular and improper. Issue 2: Proof of debt and affidavit requirements Rule applied: The Insolvency Ordinance and section 438 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the Civil Procedure Code does not govern proceedings under the Insolvency Ordinance. Therefore, the affidavit proving the debt need not comply with section 438 of the Code, provided it follows the form prescribed in the schedule to the Insolvency Ordinance. Finding: The proof of debt was sufficient as it complied with the Insolvency Ordinance. Issue 3: Validity of petition by a partner Rule applied: The Insolvency Ordinance (Form B). Court's reasoning: The court held that the objections regarding the lack of a power of attorney and the failure of all partners to sign were purely technical. Under the Insolvency Ordinance, one partner may sign on behalf of himself and his partners. In the absence of any injustice to the merits of the case, the court refused to invalidate the petition based on these technicalities. Finding: The objections were too technical to warrant setting aside the adjudication. Issue 4: Description of the insolvent Rule applied: General requirements for insolvency petitions. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the petition was drawn carelessly by only providing a name and a vague address. It held that a petition must include a sufficient description and address to ensure creditors can identify the debtor. Finding: While the petition was deficient in description, it did not necessitate setting aside the adjudication in this instance. Interaction of Issues The court's findings on the technical nature of the objections regarding the petition's signature and the affidavit requirements reinforce the principle that the Insolvency Ordinance provides a self-contained procedure. By dismissing these technical challenges, the court prioritized the merits of the insolvency adjudication over procedural irregularities, while simultaneously admonishing the lower court for its own procedural failure in delaying the appeal. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. There shall be no order for costs.

Parties: In The Matter Of The Last Will And Testament Of John Aron Ferdinandus
Keywords: Probate of will application — Timeliness of caveat entry — Procedure for recalling probate — Formal order requirements for court decisions — Exparte orders and notice of appeal — Compliance with Civil Procedure Code procedure — Propriety of unnecessary court motions
Summary: Facts Louisa Karunaratna, the named executrix of the last will of John Aron Ferdinandus, filed a petition and affidavit in the District Court of Colombo seeking probate. She stated that she had no reason to believe the application would be opposed. The District Judge granted an order absolute under section 529 of the Civil Procedure Code. Six days after this order was made, Elizabeth Ferdinandus and five others filed a document in the nature of a caveat. The executrix subsequently took her oath of office and moved for the issuance of probate. The caveators appeared and opposed this motion. The Acting District Judge ordered that the original petition and affidavit be taken off the file and directed the executrix to file a fresh petition and affidavit naming the caveators as respondents. The executrix appealed against this order, contending that the caveators were not proper parties to the proceedings and that the order was effectively ex parte. Issues Whether a caveat filed after an order absolute for probate has been granted under section 529 of the Civil Procedure Code is valid. Whether the District Court was justified in ordering the removal of the petition and affidavit from the file and requiring the executrix to file a fresh petition naming the caveators as respondents. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, s. 525 Civil Procedure Code, s. 526 Civil Procedure Code, s. 529 Civil Procedure Code, s. 535 Civil Procedure Code, s. 536 Civil Procedure Code, s. 537 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the caveat Rule applied: Section 535 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that a caveat must be entered before the order absolute is made. Because the order absolute under section 529 had already been granted prior to the filing of the caveat, the caveators were too late to intervene in the proceedings. Consequently, they were not parties to the matter. Finding: The caveat was filed out of time and was ineffective. Issue 2: Propriety of the District Court's order Rule applied: Sections 536 and 537 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that the procedure adopted by the Acting District Judge, which involved removing the petition from the file and forcing the executrix to restart the process, was not supported by the Civil Procedure Code. The court noted that if probate was granted improperly, the correct legal remedy for the caveators was to apply for the recall of probate via a petition under section 537, rather than filing a caveat or seeking to restart the initial application. Finding: The order of the District Court was unjustified and contrary to the procedure prescribed by the Code. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; because the caveat was invalid (Issue 1), the caveators lacked the standing to force the executrix to amend her petition. The court's determination that the caveators were not parties to the proceedings reinforced the conclusion that the District Judge's order to restart the application was procedurally incorrect, as the proper mechanism for challenging the grant of probate was a separate application for recall under section 537. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the order of the District Court is set aside. The caveators are directed to follow the procedure prescribed by the Civil Procedure Code if they wish to challenge the grant of probate.

Keywords: Procedural irregularity in evidence recording — Legality of conviction in the alternative — Legality of arrest warrant in foreign language — Assessment of prejudice from procedural error — Dishonest receipt of stolen property — Interpretation of criminal procedure statutes
Summary: Facts The appellant, Robert Uluwitike, was convicted of dishonestly receiving or retaining a stolen gold hairpin and sentenced to six months of rigorous imprisonment. Initially, a different individual named Urudihami was charged with the theft, and evidence was recorded against him by the Acting Police Magistrate. During those proceedings, the evidence implicated the appellant, leading the Magistrate to issue a warrant for the appellant's arrest while the proceedings against Urudihami were discontinued. Upon the appellant's arrest, he was brought before the Police Court of Galle. Instead of recording the evidence of the prosecution witnesses afresh, the Magistrate read the previously recorded depositions from the Urudihami case to the witnesses in the presence of the appellant. The witnesses were then examined further, and the appellant was granted an opportunity to cross-examine them on the entirety of the evidence. The appellant challenged the conviction on the grounds of procedural irregularity and insufficiency of evidence. Issues Whether the procedure of reading previously recorded evidence from a trial against a different accused to witnesses in the presence of the appellant constitutes a fatal irregularity. Whether a conviction in the alternative for "dishonestly receiving or retaining" stolen property is legally permissible under the Penal Code. Whether the issuance of a warrant of arrest in the Sinhalese language affects the legality of the arrest. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 22 of 1890, s. 12 Criminal Procedure Code, s. 472 Penal Code Criminal Procedure Code, s. 210 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Procedural Irregularity in Recording Evidence Rule applied: Section 12 of Ordinance No. 22 of 1890 and Section 472 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that Section 12 of Ordinance No. 22 of 1890, which allows for the reading of depositions in the presence of an accused, is intended to apply only to cases where the accused is the same person against whom the evidence was originally recorded. Because the evidence in this case was originally given in a proceeding against a different person (Urudihami), the procedure was irregular. However, the court determined that this irregularity did not prejudice the appellant, as he was given the opportunity to cross-examine the witnesses on the evidence presented. Finding: The procedure was irregular but not sufficient to set aside the conviction. Issue 2: Legality of Alternative Conviction Rule applied: The Penal Code. Court's reasoning: The court noted that while the Indian Penal Code allows for convictions in the alternative, the local Penal Code does not contain a corresponding provision. The inclusion of such a clause in the Criminal Procedure Code was an error, as the substantive Penal Code lacks the necessary authority. Consequently, the conviction for "receiving or retaining" was improper. Finding: The conviction was irregular, but the court exercised its power to amend the conviction by striking out the words "or retaining." Issue 3: Legality of Warrant in Sinhalese Rule applied: The language of the Courts (English). Court's reasoning: The court observed that the warrant was issued in the Sinhalese language. It stated that the language of the courts is English and expressed serious doubts regarding the legality of an arrest executed under a warrant issued in a foreign language. Finding: The court noted the illegality but did not invalidate the conviction on this basis, focusing instead on the merits of the case. Interaction of Issues The court's findings on the procedural irregularities regarding evidence and the alternative conviction were treated as technical defects that did not undermine the substantive finding of guilt. The court resolved the issues by amending the conviction and affirming the sentence, thereby prioritizing the merits of the evidence over the procedural flaws. Conclusion The conviction is amended by striking out the words "or retaining," and the sentence of six months' rigorous imprisonment is affirmed. The appeal is otherwise dismissed.

Keywords: Thesawalamai property classification — Muthusam vs. Thediathettum — Status of land purchased with inherited money — Ear-marking of inherited funds — Spouses' separate property rights — Rights of heirs to marital property under Thesawalamai — Procedural appeal against issue framing
Summary: Facts The plaintiff brought an action against the defendants seeking a declaration of title, restoration of possession, and damages regarding a parcel of land. The plaintiff claimed that the land was originally owned by one Tamotharam and subsequently sold by his executor, Selvanayagam, to one Suppramanian Chetty. The land was later re-sold by Suppramanian Chetty to Selvanayagam in his private capacity. The plaintiff alleged that Selvanayagam purchased the land using money inherited from his mother and that upon Selvanayagam's death, his executor sold the land to the plaintiff. The defendants admitted the sale to Selvanayagam but denied that the purchase was made using inherited money. They contended that the land was acquired during Selvanayagam's marriage to his first wife, Tangamuttu, and thus constituted joint acquired property, or thediathettum. The District Judge refused to frame an issue regarding whether the land was purchased with inherited money or joint funds, holding that property acquired by re-investing inherited cash does not retain the character of inherited property. The plaintiff appealed this refusal. Issues Whether land purchased during marriage with money inherited by one spouse constitutes the separate property of that spouse or part of the thediathettum under the Thesawalamai. Whether the District Judge erred in refusing to frame an issue regarding the source of funds used to purchase the land in question. Applicable Law Statutes: Thesawalamai of Jaffna (Chapter IV, section 3) Cases Referred to: Toussaint v. Vesentipulle - [Mutukistna-325] Valliama v. Loopen - [Mutukistna-260] Sanmogam v. Sinnecooty - [Mutukistna-33] Analysis Issue 1: Characterization of property purchased with inherited funds Rule applied: The court applied the principles of the Thesawalamai, specifically interpreting the distinction between separate property (muthusam/chidanam) and thediathettum (acquired property). Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that while thediathettum generally comprises property acquired during marriage, it does not include property acquired through the transmutation of separate, inherited assets. The court held that if a spouse purchases land using money specifically inherited or donated to them, that land retains the character of separate property. The common estate is limited to the rents, revenue, and income of separate estates and property acquired through the joint exertions of the spouses. Finding: Land purchased with money inherited by a spouse is the separate property of that spouse, provided the funds can be clearly identified or "ear-marked." Issue 2: Refusal to frame the issue Rule applied: The court applied the procedural requirement to determine the factual basis of property acquisition to resolve disputes under the Thesawalamai. Court's reasoning: The District Judge had erroneously concluded that the re-investment of inherited cash into land automatically converted it into thediathettum. The appellate court found this interpretation incorrect. Because the character of the property (separate vs. joint) depended entirely on the source of the purchase money, the issue of whether the funds were inherited or joint was essential to the resolution of the case. Finding: The District Judge erred in refusing to frame the issue; the case must be remanded to determine the source of the funds. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent. The resolution of the legal status of the property (Issue 1) necessitated the determination of the factual source of the funds (Issue 2). Without the factual finding on the source of the money, the court could not apply the legal rule regarding the exclusion of inherited assets from the thediathettum. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the order of the District Judge is set aside. The case is remanded for trial to determine whether the land was purchased with money specifically inherited by Selvanayagam, as the land is to be treated as his separate property only if such inheritance is proven.

Keywords: Application of Civil Procedure Code section 642 proviso — Representation of deceased mortgagor's estate — Retrospective application of procedural law — Mortgage debt recovery action — Judicial discretion in appointing estate representative — Distinction between administration for small and large estates — Hypothecary action against deceased's estate
Summary: Facts The Appellant, acting as the executrix of the estate of Jeronis Pieris, sought to initiate a hypothecary action against the estate of a deceased mortgagor, R. D. Thomas, to recover a loan of Rs. 500 secured by a mortgage bond. The Appellant moved the District Court of Colombo to appoint the widow of the deceased mortgagor to represent his estate for the purposes of the action. The Acting District Judge disallowed the motion on the grounds that the mortgagor had died before the Civil Procedure Code came into operation, relying on a previous judicial ruling. The Appellant appealed this decision. Issues Whether the proviso to Section 642 of the Civil Procedure Code allows the District Court to appoint a representative for a deceased mortgagor's estate where the mortgage value is less than Rs. 1,000, regardless of whether the mortgagor died before the Code came into operation. Whether the administration provisions of the Civil Procedure Code are a prerequisite for the appointment of a representative under the proviso to Section 642. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 642 Civil Procedure Code, Chapter XXXVIII Cases Referred to: Nagappen v. Maimanchy - [9-scc-197] Muttupillai v. Sellamma - [9-scc-179] 1,422, C. R., Kalutara - [sc-m-14th-may-1895] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Section 642 to past deaths Rule applied: The proviso to Section 642 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court distinguished the present application from cases involving estates valued over Rs. 1,000, which require formal administration under Chapter XXXVIII. The Court reasoned that the proviso to Section 642 is an independent procedure intended to facilitate the recovery of smaller mortgage debts. Because the language of the proviso does not restrict its application to deaths occurring after the enactment of the Code, and because the purpose of the Code is to regulate procedure for both past and present matters, the Court held that the date of the mortgagor's death does not bar the application of this specific procedure. Finding: Yes, the District Court has the authority to appoint a representative for the estate of a mortgagor who died before the Code came into operation, provided the mortgage value is under Rs. 1,000. Issue 2: Independence of the proviso Rule applied: Section 642 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court clarified that Section 642 addresses two distinct scenarios. For estates valued at Rs. 1,000 or more, a mortgagee must procure an administrator under Chapter XXXVIII, which is limited to cases where the deceased died after the Code's enactment. However, for estates valued under Rs. 1,000, the proviso allows for a representative to be appointed specifically for the purposes of the action without needing to invoke the testamentary chapters of the Code. Finding: The proviso to Section 642 is independent of the testamentary chapters of the Code, and compliance with the administration provisions is not required for appointments under the proviso. Interaction of Issues The findings reinforce each other by establishing that the procedural requirements for mortgage actions are bifurcated based on the value of the estate. By determining that the proviso is independent of the restrictive testamentary chapters, the Court confirms that the procedural hurdle regarding the date of death—which applies to formal administration—does not impede the simpler, representative-based procedure intended for smaller mortgage debts. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the order of the District Judge is set aside. The District Court is directed to exercise its judicial discretion to determine whether it is necessary or desirable to appoint a representative for the deceased mortgagor's estate for the purposes of the action.

Bench: Withers, J; Browne, J; Lawrie, J
Keywords: Agent's personal liability for unauthorized acts — Breach of implied warranty of authority — Innocent misrepresentation by agent — Absence of fraudulent intent in agency representation — Principal's repudiation of contract — Damages for unauthorized agency — Burden of proving agent's lack of authority
Summary: Facts The plaintiffs initiated an action for damages against the defendant, alleging that he induced them to enter into a contract for the sale of a house by falsely representing that he possessed the authority to act as an agent for the Bank of Madras. The plaintiffs contended that they accepted the defendant's bids and his signature on the conditions of sale based on this warranty of authority. The Bank of Madras subsequently repudiated the contract, and the property was later sold to a third party at a lower price, resulting in the damages claimed by the plaintiffs. The defendant denied the allegations of fraud or reckless misrepresentation and asserted that he acted in the belief that he was authorized to purchase the property, which he claimed was described erroneously in the conditions of sale. The District Judge dismissed the plaintiffs' case, concluding that they failed to prove the defendant lacked the necessary authority. The plaintiffs appealed this decision. Issues Whether an agent who makes an innocent misrepresentation regarding their authority to contract on behalf of a principal is personally liable in damages to the third party. Whether the plaintiffs successfully discharged the burden of proving that the defendant lacked the authority to act on behalf of the Bank of Madras. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Smout v. Ilbery - [10-m-and-w-1] Derry v. Peek - [Not specified] Bishop v. Balkis Consold. Company - [59-l-j-q-b-565] Law v. Bouverie - [Not specified] Analysis Issue 1: Personal liability for innocent misrepresentation Rule applied: The court applied the principles established in Derry v. Peek, which clarified that an action for damages does not lie for a merely careless or innocent misrepresentation in the absence of fraud or reckless intent. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that since the plaint did not allege fraudulent intent or recklessness, the defendant could not be held liable for damages. The court distinguished the present case from earlier precedents that suggested an agent might be liable for innocent misrepresentation, noting that such views were inconsistent with the House of Lords' decision in Derry v. Peek. The court further noted that the defendant was under no legal duty to the plaintiffs to state the truth, nor did he enter into a valid contract with them. Finding: The defendant is not personally liable in damages for an innocent misrepresentation of authority. Issue 2: Proof of lack of authority Rule applied: The burden of proof rests upon the plaintiffs to establish that the defendant lacked the authority he professed to have. Court's reasoning: The court found that the plaintiffs' evidence, including the bank's repudiation and the absence of a formal power of attorney, was insufficient to conclusively prove the defendant lacked authority at the time of the bid. The court noted that the bank's refusal to purchase could have been based on other factors, such as a dispute over the description of the property, rather than a lack of initial authorization. Finding: The plaintiffs failed to discharge the burden of proving that the defendant acted without authority. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the defendant is not liable for innocent misrepresentation provides a legal basis for dismissing the claim regardless of the factual dispute over authority. Conversely, the failure of the plaintiffs to prove the lack of authority renders the question of liability for misrepresentation moot, as there was no established "false" representation to begin with. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of the District Court is affirmed. The plaintiffs are ordered to pay the costs of the action.

Bench: Burnside, J; Withers, J; Lawrie, J
Keywords: Appeal procedure from Forest Settlement Officer — Stamp duty for Supreme Court judgment — Statutory interpretation of appeal procedural requirements — Condition precedent to right of appeal — Harmonization of stamp duty legislation — Judicial discretion in procedural matters
Summary: Facts Menikhami Gamarala and others were claimants in a matter before a Forest Settlement Officer. The Forest Settlement Officer issued a decision in favour of the Crown. The claimants sought to appeal this decision to the Supreme Court. The appellants lodged a petition of appeal and an affidavit but failed to supply the proper stamp for the decree or order of the Supreme Court at the time of lodging the appeal. The Solicitor-General contended that the appeal was inadmissible due to the failure to provide the necessary stamp for the judgment in appeal as required by the prevailing practice for appeals from District Courts. Issues Whether the requirement under the Stamp Ordinance to deliver a stamp for the Supreme Court decree at the time of lodging an appeal applies to appeals from a Forest Settlement Officer under Section 9 of the Forest Ordinance No. 1 of 1892. Whether the failure to supply the stamp for the judgment in appeal at the time of lodging the petition renders the appeal inadmissible. Applicable Law Statutes: Forest Ordinance No. 1 of 1892, s. 9, sub-section 6 Stamp Ordinance No. 3 of 1890 Civil Procedure Code, s. 750 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Stamp Ordinance requirements Rule applied: Section 9, sub-section 6 of the Forest Ordinance No. 1 of 1892. Court's reasoning: The Court held that while the Forest Ordinance mandates that stamp "rates" for subsequent proceedings shall be the same as those for appeals from District Courts, it does not incorporate the specific procedural requirements of the Stamp Ordinance regarding the "time" when such stamps must be delivered. The Court noted that the Stamp Ordinance provision requires delivery to a secretary of a District Court or clerk of a Court of Requests, which has no direct equivalent in the Forest Ordinance, making the literal application of that provision impossible. Finding: The specific procedural requirement to deliver the stamp for the judgment at the time of lodging the appeal does not apply to appeals under the Forest Ordinance. Issue 2: Admissibility of the appeal Rule applied: Section 9 of the Forest Ordinance No. 1 of 1892. Court's reasoning: The Court determined that a judgment in appeal is a "subsequent proceeding" and must be stamped at the rates specified for District Court appeals. However, because the Ordinance is silent on the timing of this payment, the Court exercised its inherent power to regulate proceedings to ensure justice. The Court concluded that the appeal should not be rejected for this omission, provided that counsel undertakes to supply the necessary stamps before the argument of the appeal. Finding: The appeal is admissible provided that counsel gives an undertaking to supply the proper stamps for the judgment in appeal. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the Court's determination that the strict procedural timing of the Stamp Ordinance does not apply (Issue 1) necessitated the creation of a judicial practice to ensure the revenue is protected (Issue 2). By requiring an undertaking from counsel, the Court balanced the statutory right of appeal with the administrative necessity of ensuring that subsequent proceedings are properly stamped. Conclusion The Court held that the appeal is admissible despite the failure to supply the stamp at the time of filing. The appeal shall proceed upon the undertaking of counsel that the proper stamps for the judgment in appeal will be supplied.

Parties: Dona Maria v. Don Paules De Silva
Keywords: Community of property (Roman-Dutch Law) — Administration of intestate estates — Rights of surviving spouse — Scope of administratrix's powers — Liability for community debts — Procedural efficiency in estate administration — Avoidance of multiplicity of suits
Summary: Facts Eusebias Perera and Dona Maria were married in community of property in 1866. In 1882, Eusebias Perera entered into a lease for an undivided half share of land containing plumbago pits, with the lease term commencing in 1885. Eusebias Perera died in 1884, and his widow, Dona Maria, obtained letters of administration for the entirety of his estate in 1885. Dona Maria subsequently initiated an action against the defendant, Don Paules de Silva, seeking an account of the plumbago extracted from the pits and a moiety of the net proceeds. The defendant contended that upon the death of the husband, the community of property ceased and the widow became entitled to one-half of the leasehold by operation of law. The defendant argued that the plaintiff, as administratrix, could only claim an account for the remaining one-fourth share, as her own half share had already vested in her personally and was not subject to administration. The District Judge overruled the defendant's objection, holding that the whole estate must be administered to satisfy debts and facilitate proper distribution. The defendant appealed this decision. Issues Whether the surviving spouse’s half share of property held in community of property vests in her immediately upon the death of the other spouse, thereby excluding it from the administration of the deceased’s estate. Whether an administrator is entitled to administer the whole of the estate, including the share of the surviving spouse, to ensure the satisfaction of debts and avoid a multiplicity of suits. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Vesting of the surviving spouse's share Rule applied: Roman-Dutch Law regarding community of property. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged the general principle of Roman-Dutch Law that a surviving spouse acquires a right to one-half of the property held in community upon the death of the other spouse. However, the court reasoned that this right is qualified by the surviving spouse's liability for the debts contracted during the marriage. The court determined that the widow's share cannot be separated or divided until the debts of the estate are satisfied and the total value of the estate is ascertained. Finding: While the widow has a right to a moiety, that right is subject to the administration of the whole estate for the purpose of settling liabilities. Issue 2: Authority of the administrator Rule applied: The law of administration as engrafted upon the Roman-Dutch Law. Court's reasoning: The court held that the appointment of an administrator is intended to provide a responsible party to manage the disposal of an intestate's estate among creditors and next of kin. Burnside, C.J., emphasized that it would be a "gross anomaly" if an administrator, who is liable to be sued for the deceased's debts, could not realize the property necessary to satisfy those debts. Furthermore, the court noted that allowing the defendant's contention would lead to a multiplicity of suits, as the widow would be forced to litigate in her dual capacity as an individual and as an administratrix. Finding: The administrator is entitled and required to take control of the whole estate, including the share held in community, to ensure efficient administration and debt satisfaction. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent; the court’s determination that the administrator must control the whole estate (Issue 2) effectively qualifies the immediate vesting of the widow's share (Issue 1). By prioritizing the procedural necessity of settling debts and avoiding multiple lawsuits, the court concluded that the administrative process must encompass the entire community estate, thereby rendering the defendant's objection regarding the widow's personal share legally insufficient. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of the District Court is affirmed. The court held that the administrator is entitled to administer the whole of the estate to ensure the proper discharge of debts and the orderly distribution of assets.

Keywords: Application for certificate to appeal to Privy Council — Determination of appealable value — Plaint's stated value as decisive — Requirements for appeal under Courts Ordinance — Framing of issues in civil procedure — Claim for injunction and damages
Summary: Facts The plaintiffs initiated an action against the defendant to obtain an injunction restraining the defendant from trading under the name and style of Delmege, Reid & Co. and to recover Rs. 9,000 as damages. The plaintiffs did not assign a specific value to the right to use the name in their plaint. The District Court refused the injunction and dismissed the plaintiffs' case on the basis that no damages were proved. Upon appeal, the Supreme Court affirmed the dismissal of the action. The plaintiffs subsequently applied to the Supreme Court for a certificate under section 781 of the Civil Procedure Code to appeal to the Privy Council, asserting that the case met the requirements of section 42 of the Courts Ordinance, 1889, regarding the value of the subject-matter in issue. Issues Whether the value of the subject-matter of a suit for the purpose of granting a certificate for appeal to the Privy Council is determined by the statement in the plaint, regardless of whether such value was proven at trial. Whether a judgment dismissing an action for failure to prove damages constitutes a judgment in respect of a sum at issue exceeding the statutory threshold of Rs. 5,000. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, s. 40 Civil Procedure Code, s. 146 Civil Procedure Code, s. 781 Courts Ordinance, 1889, s. 42 Cases Referred to: Hadjiar v. Pitchey - [D. C. Colombo, 2,298] Analysis Issue 1: Determination of value Rule applied: Section 42 of the Courts Ordinance, 1889. Court's reasoning: The Court examined whether the value of the subject-matter must be established by evidence or if the claim in the pleadings suffices. Relying on the precedent set in Hadjiar v. Pitchey, the Court observed that the Privy Council had previously granted leave to appeal where the value of the subject-matter was based solely on the plaintiff's estimate in the plaint, even in the absence of evidence regarding that value. The Court concluded that the value of the subject-matter is to be determined by the statement in the plaint. Finding: Yes, the value of the subject-matter is determined by the statement in the plaint, regardless of whether it was proven at trial. Issue 2: Judgment in respect of sum at issue Rule applied: Section 42 of the Courts Ordinance, 1889. Court's reasoning: Although no specific issues regarding damages were framed at trial and no evidence was led, the Court reasoned that the dismissal of the action effectively precluded the plaintiffs from recovering the Rs. 9,000 claimed. Therefore, the judgment was substantively given in respect of the claim for damages, which exceeded the Rs. 5,000 threshold required by the Ordinance. Finding: Yes, the judgment was given in respect of a sum at issue exceeding Rs. 5,000. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent because the determination that the plaint's valuation governs the subject-matter (Issue 1) provides the necessary basis to conclude that the "sum at issue" in the judgment (Issue 2) meets the statutory threshold. By accepting the plaint's valuation of Rs. 9,000, the Court confirms that the judgment dismissing the claim effectively disposes of a matter valued above the Rs. 5,000 requirement. Conclusion The application for the certificate under section 781 of the Civil Procedure Code is granted. The plaintiffs are entitled to the certificate as the case fulfills the requirements of section 42 of the Courts Ordinance, 1889.

Keywords: Right of appeal from summary conviction on admission of guilt — Strict compliance with recording accused's admission — Status and powers of Additional Police Magistrates — Continuity of judicial proceedings by a single Magistrate — Wise exercise of Magistrate's discretion — Statutory interpretation of repealed criminal procedure provisions
Summary: Facts The accused, Arnolis, was charged with keeping a common gaming-house in breach of section 5 of Ordinance No. 17 of 1889. Upon being brought before the Police Court, the particulars of the offence were explained to him. The record indicated that the accused pleaded guilty to the charge, and he was subsequently convicted on that plea. The accused sought to appeal the conviction, but the Magistrate rejected the appeal, citing section 403 of the Criminal Procedure Code, which prohibited appeals where an accused had pleaded guilty. The proceedings were titled as being before the Additional Police Court of Colombo. Following a previous order by the Supreme Court to discharge the accused, the Magistrate who had originally convicted him, while sitting at Henaratgoda, ordered the accused to be brought from the Colombo jail to Henaratgoda to be discharged. Upon his discharge, the accused was immediately re-charged with the same offence by a police officer, and the Magistrate ordered him to find bail, resulting in his return to custody. Issues Whether the statutory bar to an appeal under section 403 of the Criminal Procedure Code applies to summary convictions in Police Courts following the repeal of section 229 of the Code. Whether the Magistrate complied with the requirements of section 220 of the Criminal Procedure Code regarding the recording of an accused's admission of guilt. Whether the designation "Additional Police Court" creates a distinct and independent court under Ordinance No. 1 of 1889. Whether the Magistrate exercised wise discretion in entertaining a complaint and ordering bail at a location distant from the accused's residence, causing unnecessary hardship. Applicable Law Statutes: Criminal Procedure Code, s. 220 Criminal Procedure Code, s. 229 (repealed) Criminal Procedure Code, s. 403 Ordinance No. 1 of 1889, ss. 55, 56, 57, 89 Ordinance No. 17 of 1889, s. 5 Ordinance No. 22 of 1890 Cases Referred to: 1 C. L. R. 14 - [1-clr-14] Analysis Issue 1: Right of appeal Rule applied: Section 403 of the Criminal Procedure Code and the amending Ordinance No. 22 of 1890. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that section 403 was predicated on the procedure in the original Code, specifically section 229, which provided for a formal plea of guilty. Since the 1890 amendment repealed section 229 and replaced it with a summary procedure under section 220, section 403 has been rendered inapplicable to Police Court summary trials. Finding: There is no statutory bar to an appeal by an accused person convicted summarily in a Police Court upon an admission of guilt. Issue 2: Recording of admission Rule applied: Section 220 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that the Magistrate must record the admission of an offence as nearly as possible in the exact words used by the accused. Recording merely that the accused "pleaded guilty" is insufficient, as the court must be able to verify that the accused understood the specific elements of the statutory offence. Finding: The conviction must be quashed because the Magistrate failed to record the accused's actual words, preventing the appellate court from determining if the plea was informed. Issue 3: Status of Additional Police Courts Rule applied: Ordinance No. 1 of 1889, sections 55, 56, and 57. Court's reasoning: The court clarified that the Governor may appoint multiple Magistrates to a single Police Court, but they all constitute one and the same court. There is no legal basis for the existence of an "Additional Police Court" as a separate entity. Finding: Proceedings should be titled as being in the Police Court of the division, before one of the Magistrates of said court. Issue 4: Exercise of discretion Rule applied: General principles of judicial discretion. Court's reasoning: While the Magistrate had the legal jurisdiction to entertain the complaint, the court found that doing so at Henaratgoda, far from the accused's home, caused unnecessary hardship. The Magistrate should have exercised discretion by referring the complainant to the Colombo Police Court. Finding: The Magistrate did not exercise wise discretion, as the circumstances did not necessitate immediate action at a distant location. Interaction of Issues The findings on the status of the court and the procedural requirements for recording pleas reinforce the court's supervisory role over summary proceedings. The failure to properly record the plea (Issue 2) necessitated the quashing of the conviction, while the clarification of court status (Issue 3) and the critique of judicial discretion (Issue 4) serve to prevent future procedural irregularities and hardships in the administration of justice. Conclusion The conviction is quashed. The court held that there is no statutory bar to an appeal in these circumstances and that the Magistrate failed to comply with the mandatory recording requirements of the Criminal Procedure Code.

Parties: The Queen v. Anisu Lebbe Et Al.
Keywords: Admissibility of accused's statements and answers — Proof requirements for evidence from preliminary inquiry — Irregular reception of evidence and conviction — Effect of improperly admitted evidence — Interpretation of criminal procedure provisions — Accused's right to procedural compliance — Non-summary inquiry procedure
Summary: Facts Four men were tried and convicted in the District Court of Batticaloa on a charge of robbing a temple. During the trial, the prosecution sought to introduce into evidence certain questions put to the first and second accused by the Police Magistrate during a non-summary inquiry, along with the corresponding answers provided by the accused. These questions and answers were recorded under section 16 of Ordinance No. 1 of 1888. The prosecution introduced these records by simply producing the documents, which purported to be signed and certified by the Magistrate. No witness was called to testify that these questions were actually put to the accused or that the answers were indeed made by them. The District Judge admitted this evidence, and the accused were subsequently convicted. Issues Whether questions and answers recorded under section 16 of Ordinance No. 1 of 1888 are admissible in evidence at a trial without independent proof of their authenticity. Whether the irregular admission of such evidence constitutes sufficient grounds to quash a conviction. Applicable Law Statutes: Criminal Procedure Code Ordinance No. 1 of 1888 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Admissibility of section 16 records Rule applied: Section 16 of Ordinance No. 1 of 1888 and section 473 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court observed that while section 15 of Ordinance No. 1 of 1888 provides that statements and confessions recorded under section 136 of the Criminal Procedure Code are admissible without further proof, no such provision exists for questions and answers recorded under section 16 of Ordinance No. 1 of 1888. Consequently, these records do not benefit from a presumption of admissibility. To be admitted, they must be proved under the general law by a person who was present at the inquiry and heard the questions and answers. Finding: The questions and answers were wrongly received in evidence because they were not properly proved. Issue 2: Effect of irregular evidence on conviction Rule applied: General principles of criminal procedure regarding the reception of evidence. Court's reasoning: The court held that the accused are entitled to have the formalities of the law strictly complied with. Because the improperly recorded evidence was admitted, the court could not determine the extent to which this evidence influenced the District Judge's mind. The court refused to speculate on whether the remaining evidence was sufficient to sustain the conviction, noting that it could not simply eliminate the tainted evidence and uphold the verdict. Finding: The irregular reception of the evidence is fatal to the conviction. Conclusion The conviction is quashed, and the case is remitted to the District Court for a new trial with assessors. The court held that the failure to properly prove the questions and answers recorded under section 16 of Ordinance No. 1 of 1888 rendered their admission irregular and prejudicial.

Keywords: Specific performance of agreement to sell land — Breach of contract for sale of immovable property — Alternative claim for damages — Doctrine of specific performance in Sri Lankan law — Simultaneous performance of reciprocal contractual obligations — Legal tender of purchase price
Summary: Facts The plaintiff initiated an action for specific performance or, in the alternative, damages against the defendant. The dispute arose from a deed of agreement dated 6th June 1893, between the defendant and one Wapuchi Marikar, wherein the defendant agreed to sell a portion of land for Rs. 150. A sum of Rs. 50 was paid in advance, and it was stipulated that if the defendant failed to convey the land, he would refund the Rs. 50 and pay an additional Rs. 50 as damages. Wapuchi Marikar subsequently assigned his rights under this agreement to the plaintiff. The plaintiff alleged that both he and his assignor had requested the defendant to execute the conveyance and had expressed readiness to pay the balance of Rs. 150. The defendant refused to execute the transfer or provide the title deeds. The District Court initially ruled in favor of the plaintiff on the question of law regarding specific performance but later dismissed the action on the merits, concluding that the plaintiff had failed to prove a legal tender of the purchase price within the stipulated time. The plaintiff appealed this decision. Issues Whether the doctrine of specific performance is recognized under the law of Ceylon. Whether the tender of the purchase price was a condition precedent to the defendant's obligation to execute a conveyance. Whether the plaintiff is entitled to specific performance or, in the alternative, damages for breach of contract. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 331 Cases Referred to: [Morgan and Beling 145] [Ramanathan 152] [Grenier D. C. 39] S. C. M. 19th November 1886, D. C. Negombo 14,007 - [not specified] Analysis Issue 1: Recognition of specific performance Rule applied: The court applied the Roman-Dutch law principles as articulated by Van der Linden and Grotius, alongside established local judicial practice. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the right to compel specific performance has been consistently recognized in the courts of Ceylon since 1837. The court referenced Van der Linden’s Institutes and Grotius’s Jurisprudence of Holland, which confirm that a creditor may compel a debtor to perform an act or pay damages. Furthermore, the court observed that the Civil Procedure Code assumes the existence of this remedy. Finding: The doctrine of specific performance is well-established and enforceable under the law of Ceylon. Issue 2: Tender as a condition precedent Rule applied: The law of contract regarding simultaneous obligations. Court's reasoning: The court interpreted the agreement as requiring simultaneous performance: the defendant was to provide the conveyance while the plaintiff was to provide the purchase price. The court rejected the defendant's argument that the plaintiff was required to tender the money as a condition precedent to the defendant's duty to act. The court held that the plaintiff only needed to demonstrate readiness and willingness to perform his part of the bargain. Finding: The tender of the purchase price was not a condition precedent; the plaintiff was only required to show he was ready and able to pay upon the defendant's execution of the transfer. Issue 3: Entitlement to relief Rule applied: The principles of equity and contract law regarding breach of agreement. Court's reasoning: The court found that the defendant failed to fulfill his obligation to convey the land. Since the plaintiff established his readiness to perform, he is entitled to the primary remedy of specific performance. The court provided for an alternative remedy of damages (Rs. 100) should the conveyance prove impossible for any valid reason. Finding: The plaintiff is entitled to an order for specific performance, with an alternative award of damages if the conveyance cannot be executed. Interaction of Issues The court's determination that specific performance is a valid legal remedy (Issue 1) provided the framework for the court to grant the primary relief sought. The finding that tender was not a condition precedent (Issue 2) effectively removed the barrier created by the District Court’s factual finding, allowing the court to grant the specific performance requested (Issue 3). Conclusion The judgment of the District Court is set aside. The defendant is ordered to execute a conveyance of the land in favor of the plaintiff, subject to the plaintiff paying the balance of Rs. 150 into court. If the conveyance cannot be executed for any valid reason, the defendant is ordered to pay the plaintiff Rs. 100 as damages.

Judgment by: Browne, A.J.; Withers, J.; Lawrie, J
Bench: Browne, J; Withers, J; Lawrie, J
Keywords: Prescriptive title to land — Uninterrupted and undisturbed possession — Interpretation of "interruption" of possession — Interpretation of "disturbance" of possession — Adverse possession claims — Effect of prior litigation on prescriptive period — Proof of continuous physical possession
Summary: Facts The plaintiff initiated an action seeking a declaration that his execution-debtors were the owners of a disputed property and that the defendant's claim to the land was groundless. The plaintiff derived his interest through a series of mortgages and a Fiscal's sale. The defendant contested the claim by asserting that the land originally belonged to the Crown and that he had obtained a certificate of title. Furthermore, the defendant pleaded that he had been in undisturbed and uninterrupted possession of the land for over ten years, thereby acquiring title by prescription under the Prescription Ordinance. The District Court judge ruled in favor of the plaintiff, finding that previous judgments against the defendant regarding the same land constituted res judicata and that the defendant's possession had been interrupted by various disputes, lawsuits, and acts of violence. The defendant appealed this decision. Issues Whether the previous judgments in District Court cases 41,552 and 69,316 operated as res judicata to estop the defendant from asserting his prescriptive title. Whether the defendant’s possession of the land was "uninterrupted and undisturbed" for the purposes of the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 22 of 1871, s. 3 Ordinance No. 12 of 1840, s. 7 Civil Procedure Code, s. 241 Cases Referred to: [12,911, D. C, Kurunegala] [9,601, D. C, Jaffna] [37,705, D. C, Galle] [2-c-l-r-103] [19,802, D. C, Matara] Analysis Issue 1: Res Judicata Rule applied: The principles of res judicata regarding previous litigation between the parties or their predecessors in title. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the plaintiff did not base his claim on the previous decisions in his original plaint. Furthermore, the defendant was never removed from possession by legal process following those judgments. Even if he had been, sufficient time had elapsed between those judgments and the current action for the defendant to acquire a new prescriptive title. Finding: The plea of res judicata was rejected; the previous judgments did not estop the defendant from asserting his current prescriptive title. Issue 2: Uninterrupted and Undisturbed Possession Rule applied: Section 3 of the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Court's reasoning: The court defined "interruption" as the physical removal of a possessor or the occupation of the land by a disputant. "Disturbance" was defined as acts preventing the full use of the land or converting continuous use into divided use. The court found that while the defendant was involved in lawsuits and violent confrontations, he successfully resisted all attempts to oust him. The court held that mere legal challenges or unsuccessful attempts at violence do not constitute an interruption of possession if the possessor remains in physical control. Finding: The defendant’s possession was found to be continuous, uninterrupted, and undisturbed for the statutory period. Interaction of Issues The court's finding on the lack of interruption (Issue 2) reinforced the rejection of the res judicata claim (Issue 1). Because the previous legal actions did not result in the defendant's eviction, they failed to serve as either a legal bar to his title or a factual interruption of his prescriptive possession. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the District Court is set aside. The plaintiff's action is dismissed with costs.

Keywords: Right to sue of voluntary association — Locus standi of individual representing association — Procedural technicalities in Courts of Requests — Admissibility of documentary evidence objection — Proper mode of raising legal objections — Representative actions for numerous members — Deprivation of costs for technicalities
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff, acting as the Honorary Secretary of the Colombo Library, initiated an action in the Court of Requests of Colombo against the Defendant, a member of the library. The Plaintiff sought to recover unpaid monthly subscriptions and the value of unreturned books. The Defendant filed an answer denying the Plaintiff's right to maintain the action. During the trial, the Plaintiff attempted to introduce the rules of the library as evidence, but the Defendant objected on the grounds that the rules were not included in the list of documents filed by the Plaintiff. The Commissioner of the Court of Requests overruled the Defendant's objection regarding the Plaintiff's standing, suggesting that such an objection should have been raised by a motion to take the plaint off the file rather than in the answer. The Commissioner ultimately entered judgment for the Plaintiff. The Defendant appealed the decision. Issues Whether the Commissioner of the Court of Requests erred in his procedural handling of the objection regarding the Plaintiff's right to sue. Whether the Honorary Secretary of a voluntary association has the legal standing to maintain an action on behalf of the association against a member. Whether the Defendant's objection to the admissibility of the library rules was justified. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, s. 10 Civil Procedure Code, s. 16 Cases Referred to: Evans v. Hooper - [11-q-b-d-45] Gray v. Pearson - [5-c-l-r-568] Jones v. Chennell - [8-ch-d-506] Analysis Issue 1: Procedural handling of the objection Rule applied: Civil Procedure Code, s. 10. Court's reasoning: The Court held that there is no provision for a motion to take a plaint off the file. It is the duty of the Court to scrutinize the plaint upon filing. If a plaint is defective, the Court should reject or return it for amendment. If the plaint is valid on its face, any objection to the Plaintiff's right to sue must be raised in the answer. Finding: The Commissioner erred in suggesting that the objection should have been raised by a motion to take the plaint off the file; the Defendant correctly raised the issue in his answer. Issue 2: Standing of the Honorary Secretary Rule applied: Principles regarding voluntary associations as established in Evans v. Hooper and Gray v. Pearson. Court's reasoning: The Court determined that a voluntary association cannot, by mere agreement among its members, confer upon an individual the right to sue on behalf of the members. The Court noted that Section 16 of the Civil Procedure Code provides the proper mechanism for such cases, allowing the Court to permit one or more members to sue on behalf of a numerous body. Finding: The Plaintiff lacked the legal standing to maintain the action in his capacity as Honorary Secretary. Issue 3: Admissibility of library rules Rule applied: General principles of judicial administration and the avoidance of technicalities in the Court of Requests. Court's reasoning: The Court criticized the upholding of the technical objection regarding the non-inclusion of the rules in the document list. Citing Jones v. Chennell, the Court emphasized that a judge should not allow technical difficulties to obstruct the administration of justice and should instead remove such obstacles, potentially through adjournments or orders regarding costs. Finding: The objection was purely technical and should not have been upheld; the Defendant's insistence on this point warranted a deprivation of costs in the court below. Interaction of Issues The procedural findings clarify that while the Defendant was correct to challenge the Plaintiff's standing via the answer, his conduct regarding the admissibility of evidence was obstructive. The resolution of the standing issue (Issue 2) rendered the action unsustainable, while the findings on the technical objection (Issue 3) directly influenced the Court's decision to deny the Defendant costs in the lower court. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the Plaintiff's action is dismissed. The Defendant is awarded the costs of the appeal, but is deprived of his costs in the Court below due to his conduct regarding the admissibility of evidence.

Keywords: Intentionally giving false evidence — Propriety of judge trying offence committed before him — Allegations of judicial bias — Role of assessors in criminal trials — Appellate review of conviction
Summary: Facts The accused was charged with and found guilty of intentionally giving false evidence. The offense occurred during a prior criminal proceeding, specifically case number 12,130, which had been heard and determined by the same District Judge, Mr. H. L. Moysey, on the 5th of June, 1895. Following the conviction on the 10th of October, 1895, the District Judge sentenced the accused to two years of rigorous imprisonment. The accused appealed the conviction and sentence, arguing that the trial was improper because it was presided over by the same judge who had heard the original testimony alleged to be false. Issues Whether it is legally improper for a District Judge to try an accused for the offense of intentionally giving false evidence when that evidence was given in a case previously heard by the same judge. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Propriety of the trial judge Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a judge may preside over a trial for perjury even if the false evidence was given in a prior proceeding before that same judge, provided the committal was duly made. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged the appellant's argument that a judge might possess a bias having already formed an opinion on the veracity of the testimony in the prior case. However, the court reasoned that this is a risk inherent in judicial inquiries. The court drew an analogy to jury trials, where jurors are often required to determine the guilt of an accused regarding contradictory statements they have previously heard. While the court expressed a preference that the District Judge should have utilized the assistance of assessors to ensure a more satisfactory process, it concluded that the trial had been conducted with sufficient care and patience. Finding: The court found that the trial was not legally improper and that the conviction and sentence should not be disturbed. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The conviction and the sentence of two years' rigorous imprisonment are upheld.

Keywords: Minor's capacity to act as agent — Validity of power of attorney issued to minor — Scope of 'recognized agent' under Civil Procedure Code — Suit instituted by minor holding power of attorney — Setting aside warrant in mesne process — Interpretation of Civil Procedure Code sections on agency — Delegated authority without sui juris requirement
Summary: Facts The plaintiff, Palaniappa Chetty, instituted an action against the defendant, Ibrahim Saibu, to recover money due on a promissory note. The suit was filed through the plaintiff's attorney, Somasundaram Chetty, who held a general power of attorney. During the proceedings, the plaintiff obtained a warrant in mesne process against the defendant under section 650 of the Civil Procedure Code, leading to the defendant's arrest and subsequent release upon providing a security bond. The defendant moved to have the plaint struck off the file and to be discharged from the security bond. The defendant argued that the debt had been settled, that the attorney Somasundaram Chetty was a minor under twenty-one years of age, and that the allegations supporting the warrant were false. The District Judge allowed the motion on the basis of the attorney's minority, and the plaintiff appealed this order. Issues Whether a minor holding a general power of attorney is competent to act as a recognized agent for a principal under section 24 of the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, s. 24 Civil Procedure Code, s. 25 Civil Procedure Code, s. 650 Cases Referred to: Co Litt. 52 a. Analysis Issue 1: Competency of a minor as a recognized agent Rule applied: Section 24 and Section 25 of the Civil Procedure Code, and the principle that a naked authority delegated by a power of attorney may be exercised by any person of competent understanding regardless of whether they are sui juris. Court's reasoning: The court observed that Somasundaram Chetty, although twenty years of age, had been engaged in trade for three years and possessed a general power of attorney. The court reasoned that acting as a recognized agent under section 24 of the Civil Procedure Code is a form of delegation that does not impose personal liability on the agent. The agent merely serves as the mouthpiece for the principal. The court found no prohibition in the English law governing principal and agent relations that would prevent a minor of competent understanding from exercising such authority. Consequently, the court held that the attorney was competent to appear and act on behalf of his principal. Finding: The court found that a minor holding a general power of attorney is competent to act as a recognized agent for the limited purposes mentioned in section 24 of the Civil Procedure Code. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the order of the District Court is set aside. The record is remitted to the District Court for the trial to proceed in due course, with costs awarded to the plaintiff.

Keywords: Magistrate's jurisdiction over grave offences — Procedural irregularity in criminal trial — Attorney-General's powers in committal proceedings — Summary trial of lesser offence while graver charge pending — Prevention of withdrawal from proper tribunal — Integrity of witness testimony — Interconnectedness of criminal offences
Summary: Facts The accused appellant was charged with intentional insult under section 484 of the Penal Code and criminal intimidation under section 486 of the Penal Code. The Magistrate heard evidence from the complainant and witnesses, who testified that the accused drew a knife and threatened to kill the complainant. Recognizing that the charge under section 486 was beyond his summary jurisdiction, the Magistrate formulated charges for both offences and forwarded the proceedings to the Solicitor-General pursuant to section 175 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Upon the return of the record, the Magistrate proceeded to try the accused summarily only for the offence under section 484.
