The court expressed strong suspicion that the alleged payments were fictitious and fabricated solely to support the current application for a second writ after a significant lapse of time. Finding: The failure to certify payments under Section 349, combined with the suspicious nature of the claims, renders the appellant's justification for a second writ invalid. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the court's decision to deny the application. The lack of due diligence (Issue 1) establishes a procedural failure to exhaust legal remedies, while the failure to certify payments (Issue 2) undermines the factual basis upon which the appellant sought to excuse his delay. Together, these failures demonstrate that the appellant did not meet the statutory requirements for re-issuing the writ. Conclusion The order of the Acting District Judge is affirmed. The appeal is dismissed with costs.

Parties: Muttiah Chetty v. De Silva Et Al.
Keywords: Minor's liability on promissory note — Roman-Dutch law principles of minority — Contract by filius familias with paternal consent — Emancipation of minor by trade — Restitutio in integrum as minor's remedy — Admissibility and evidentiary value of birth certificates — Calculation of interest in judgment debt
Summary: Facts The appellant was sued on a promissory note that he had executed jointly and severally with his father and a third defendant. The appellant pleaded that he was a minor at the time of the execution of the note, citing the age of majority as twenty-one years under Ordinance No. 7 of 1865. Evidence indicated that the appellant had assisted his father in a boutique business from a young age, that the business operated under a firm name including both their names, and that the note was issued for business purposes. The appellant attempted to produce a birth certificate under Ordinance No. 18 of 1867 to prove his minority, but the District Judge refused to admit it as evidence of the date of birth. The District Court entered judgment against the appellant, who subsequently appealed. Issues Whether the plea of minority constitutes a valid defense to an action on a promissory note executed by a minor with the consent of his father. Whether the interest awarded in the decree was calculated in accordance with the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Ordinance No. 18 of 1867 Ordinance No. 7 of 1865 Civil Procedure Code, s. 192 Cases Referred to: Letchiman Chetty v. Perera - [4-s-c-c-80] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the plea of minority Rule applied: Roman-Dutch Law principles regarding patria potestas and the liability of a filius familias as discussed in Censura Forensis and Voet. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that under Roman-Dutch Law, a contract entered into by an unemancipated minor with the authority of his father is binding. The court noted that the appellant’s father was a joint maker of the note, which served as evidence of his consent. Furthermore, the court observed that the remedy of restitutio in integrum is not available to a minor who incurs liabilities while practicing a trade or profession, as the lack of business experience (imperitia negotiorum) is considered absent in such circumstances. Finding: The plea of minority is not a valid defense because the note was executed with the father's consent and the liability was incurred in the course of trade. Issue 2: Calculation of interest Rule applied: Civil Procedure Code, s. 192. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the original debt merges into the decree. Therefore, interest should be calculated on the principal plus interest accrued up to the date of the action, and thereafter, the interest on the aggregate sum should be calculated at the court rate of 9 per cent rather than the contract rate of 15 per cent. Finding: The decree was improperly calculated and required amendment to reflect the aggregate sum and the correct court-mandated interest rate. Interaction of Issues The court’s finding on the first issue established the appellant's liability, which necessitated the court's intervention to correct the calculation of the judgment debt in the second issue. The resolution of the liability issue rendered the dispute over the birth certificate evidence moot, as the appellant was held liable regardless of his age at the time of the contract. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed regarding the liability of the appellant, but the decree is amended to correctly calculate the interest on the aggregate sum at 9 per cent from the date of the decree. The appellant remains liable for the debt incurred in the course of his trade.

Parties: Peris Et Al. v. Perera Et Al.,
Judgment by: Bonser, C.J.; Withers, J.; Lawrie, J
Bench: Bonser, J; Withers, J; Lawrie, J
Keywords: Conclusiveness of partition decree — Interlocutory vs. final judgment — Duty of court to ascertain all co-owners and parties — Strict proof of title in partition suits — Partition action procedure and requirements — Effect of non-joinder of necessary parties — Management of shares of unascertained co-owners — Application of res judicata to interlocutory decrees
Summary: Facts The plaintiffs claimed an undivided three-fourths share of certain lands by right of inheritance from one Naide. They alleged that the defendants were entitled to the remaining one-fourth share as descendants of one Simitchi. The plaintiffs asserted that the lands originally belonged to Adohami and his wife Bancho, and that through a specific family custom, the sons possessed the lands to the exclusion of the daughters. The plaintiffs further claimed that they were entitled to the three-fourths share of Naide to the exclusion of their own six sisters. Certain added parties claimed a divided portion of the lands. The District Judge rejected the claims of the added parties and decreed a partition as prayed for in the plaint. The added parties appealed to the Supreme Court. The Court found that the descendants of the plaintiffs' paternal aunts and the plaintiffs' own six sisters had not been made parties to the action or served with notice. Issues Whether the court is required to ensure that all persons interested in the land are parties to a partition action before entering a final decree under the Partition Ordinance No. 10 of 1863. Whether an interlocutory decree for partition under Section 4 of the Partition Ordinance No. 10 of 1863 constitutes a final and conclusive judgment binding on all parties. Whether the court may reform an interlocutory judgment if new claimants appear after the commissioner has initiated steps to prepare a return. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Ordinance No. 10 of 1863, ss. 4, 6, 9 Civil Procedure Code, ss. 18, 402, 508 Cases Referred to: Assena Marikar v. Usubu Lebbe - [1-s-c-c-10] Don Carolis v. Watta Baba - [7-s-c-c-125] Analysis Issue 1: Duty to join all interested parties Rule applied: Partition Ordinance No. 10 of 1863 and Section 18 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that a partition suit is not merely a dispute between the parties on record. The court must be satisfied that all persons entitled to or interested in the land are before it. The District Judge has a duty to conduct a non-perfunctory inquiry and use the powers under Section 18 of the Civil Procedure Code to ensure all owners are joined. The court noted that the plaintiffs' failure to include their sisters and the descendants of their paternal aunts rendered the current proceedings incomplete. Finding: The court must ensure all interested parties are joined before proceeding to a decree. Issue 2: Effect of interlocutory decree Rule applied: Section 9 of the Partition Ordinance No. 10 of 1863. Court's reasoning: The court clarified that the "decree" referred to in Section 9, which is conclusive against all persons, is the final judgment entered after the return of the commission for partition, not the interlocutory decree under Section 4. An interlocutory decree merely declares undivided shares and is not a final adjudication of title. If an interlocutory decree is not proceeded with, it cannot support a plea of res judicata and should be cleared from the rolls under Section 402 of the Civil Procedure Code. Finding: The interlocutory decree is not the final, conclusive judgment contemplated by the Ordinance. Issue 3: Reformation of interlocutory judgment Rule applied: Section 18 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that because the final judgment binds persons beyond the immediate parties, claimants who receive notice only during the commissioner's stage must be permitted to intervene. The court may hear these new claimants and, if necessary, reform the interlocutory judgment to reflect the true ownership of the land. Finding: The court may reform an interlocutory judgment to include newly discovered interested parties. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as they emphasize the court's duty to ensure the integrity of the partition process. Because the final decree is conclusive against all persons (Issue 2), the court must strictly ensure all interested parties are joined (Issue 1). The ability to reform the interlocutory judgment (Issue 3) serves as a procedural safeguard to correct omissions in the initial joinder of parties, thereby ensuring the final decree is based on a complete and accurate determination of ownership. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the partition decree is set aside. The case is remitted to the District Court for further inquiries to identify and join all necessary parties, including the descendants of the paternal aunts and the plaintiffs' sisters, before proceeding further.

Keywords: Powers of District Court in execution proceedings — Release of judgment-debtor from arrest — Alteration of decree for money payment — Interpretation of 'terms' in release order — Payment by instalments for judgment-debtor — Policy against incarceration of honest debtors — Modification of final decree
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Supermanian, sought to execute a money decree against the Respondent, Perumal, who was arrested on a writ against his person. Upon being brought before the District Court of Colombo, the District Judge issued an order allowing the Respondent to satisfy the judgment debt through monthly installments of Rs. 50. The order further stipulated that a warrant for arrest would issue only upon the Respondent's failure to pay an installment by the fifth of each month. The Appellant challenged this order, contending that the District Court lacked the authority to modify the terms of the original decree in such a manner. Issues Whether the District Court has the power under Section 300 of the Civil Procedure Code to alter the terms of a money decree to the disadvantage of the creditor when releasing a judgment-debtor. Whether an order allowing a judgment-debtor to pay a debt by installments constitutes "terms" within the meaning of Section 300 of the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Sections 300, 303, 305, 307 to 312 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Power to alter decree Rule applied: Section 300 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that while Section 300 grants the Court discretion to release a judgment-debtor upon "terms," this authority does not extend to modifying the underlying decree. The Court held that the judicial power to release a debtor is distinct from the power to adjudicate the debt itself. Altering the payment schedule from a lump sum to installments effectively reduces the creditor's immediate rights under the final decree, which the Court found to be an impermissible exercise of discretion. Finding: The District Court has no power to alter the terms of a decree to the disadvantage of the creditor. Issue 2: Definition of "terms" Rule applied: Section 300 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court interpreted the word "terms" in Section 300 to mean conditions that are onerous to the debtor, rather than the creditor. The Court observed that releasing a debtor from a lawful arrest is an indulgence granted to the debtor. Consequently, any conditions imposed must be burdensome to the debtor to justify that indulgence. An order allowing payment by installments is favorable to the debtor and detrimental to the creditor, and therefore does not satisfy the statutory requirement for "terms" under the Code. Finding: An order allowing payment by installments is not a valid "term" under Section 300 because it is not onerous to the debtor. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. By determining that the Court lacks the authority to alter the decree (Issue 1) and that "terms" must be onerous to the debtor (Issue 2), the Court established that the District Judge’s order was fundamentally flawed. The resolution of the first issue confirms the lack of jurisdiction to modify the decree, while the second issue clarifies that the specific modification attempted was substantively contrary to the legislative intent of the Civil Procedure Code. Conclusion The order of the District Court is set aside. The case is remitted to the District Court for further inquiry and determination regarding the release or committal of the debtor in accordance with the law.

Keywords: Disqualification of a Police Magistrate — Reasonable apprehension of bias — Impartiality in the administration of justice — Conflict of interest in judicial functions — Scrutiny of summary jurisdiction proceedings — Separation of police and judicial roles — Right to an impartial tribunal
Summary: Facts The Superintendent of Police for the Province of Uva was appointed as an Additional Police Magistrate of the Police Court of Badulla. Following complaints regarding street nuisances in Badulla, the Superintendent issued orders for the arrest and prosecution of offenders. A police officer, acting under these orders, arrested the appellant for committing a nuisance in his presence. When the appellant resisted the arrest, he was charged with both committing a nuisance and obstructing a police officer in the execution of his duty. The appellant was subsequently tried and convicted on both charges by the Superintendent of Police sitting in his capacity as the Additional Police Magistrate. Issues Whether the conviction of the appellant was invalid due to a reasonable apprehension of bias arising from the Magistrate’s dual role as a police officer and a judicial officer. Whether the Magistrate was disqualified from presiding over a trial involving charges of obstruction of police officers when those officers were under his direct command. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Regina v. Huggins - [1895/v1-q-b-565] Christoffelsz v. Sterna Lebbe - [1-c-l-r-5] Analysis Issue 1: Reasonable apprehension of bias Rule applied: The principle that the administration of justice by Magistrates must be free from any suspicion of unfairness, and that it is as important for justice to be believed to be unbiased as it is for it to be unbiased in fact. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the Magistrate, as the Superintendent of Police, was responsible for the peace of the district and the conduct of his subordinates. His professional interest in suppressing crime and maintaining the authority of his force created a situation where he could not maintain a thoroughly impartial attitude. The Court emphasized that the test is not whether there was actual bias, but whether there was a "reasonable apprehension of bias." The Court noted that the esprit de corps of the police force and the Magistrate's prior orders regarding these specific prosecutions made it difficult for an accused to perceive the tribunal as impartial. Finding: The conviction was held to be unsustainable due to the reasonable apprehension of bias. Issue 2: Disqualification in obstruction cases Rule applied: The principle that a police officer cannot act as a judge or investigating magistrate in cases where members of the police force are personally interested. Court's reasoning: Lawrie, J. distinguished between the charge of nuisance and the charge of obstruction. While he found no personal or pecuniary interest regarding the nuisance charge, he concluded that the Magistrate was disqualified from trying the obstruction charge. This was because the police officers involved were subordinates under the Magistrate's control, and he had a vested interest in maintaining their position and authority. Bonser, C.J. agreed that the conviction should be quashed in its entirety, noting that it is safer to enlarge the area of objection to the qualification of justices than to restrict it. Finding: The Magistrate was disqualified from presiding over the charges, particularly those involving the obstruction of his own subordinates. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the conclusion that the Magistrate’s dual capacity compromised the integrity of the proceedings. While Lawrie, J. initially considered upholding the nuisance conviction, the Court ultimately determined that the entire proceedings were tainted. The resolution of the disqualification issue regarding the obstruction charge rendered the entire trial procedurally invalid, as it was deemed inconvenient to partially sustain a conviction from a tribunal that lacked the appearance of impartiality. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the conviction is quashed. The proceedings are set aside in their entirety.

Judgment by: Bonser, C.J.; Withers, J.; Lawrie, J
Bench: Bonser, J; Withers, J; Lawrie, J
Keywords: Meaning of "Court" in civil procedure — Jurisdiction to commit or discharge judgment-debtor — Requirement for debtor to be "before the Court" — Judicial acts performed in chambers — Validity of unstamped warrant of committal — District Judge's power to re-issue arrest warrant — Quashing of irregular judicial orders
Summary: Facts The appellant, an execution-creditor, obtained a warrant for the arrest of the judgment-debtor. The warrant was executed by the Deputy Fiscal of Trincomalee, who brought the debtor to the court-house at Kandy on a Saturday afternoon. The District Judge was not holding court at that time and was present at his private residence. The Secretary of the Court informed the District Judge of the debtor's arrival and the absence of any cause shown against committal. Without the debtor being brought before him, the District Judge signed an order committing the debtor to prison. Subsequently, on the same day, the District Judge signed an order discharging the debtor on the grounds that the required stamp for the commitment warrant had not been supplied. The execution-creditor later moved the District Court to vacate the order of discharge, which the District Judge refused. The execution-creditor appealed against this refusal. Issues Whether the District Judge had the jurisdiction to order the committal or discharge of a judgment-debtor under Section 298 of the Civil Procedure Code without the debtor being physically brought before him. Whether the District Judge could exercise judicial functions, such as ordering committal or discharge, at his private residence rather than in open court. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 298 Civil Procedure Code, Section 305 Stamp Ordinance Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction to order committal or discharge Rule applied: Section 298 of the Civil Procedure Code, which requires that a judgment-debtor arrested on a warrant must be brought before the Court. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the requirement to bring the debtor "before the Court" necessitates that the debtor be brought before the Judge while the Judge is acting in a judicial capacity. The District Judge acted without the debtor being present, which rendered the orders of committal and discharge invalid. The Court emphasized that the Judge cannot exercise judicial functions regarding a person who is not physically present before him. Finding: The District Judge lacked jurisdiction to issue the orders because the debtor was not brought before him. Issue 2: Exercise of judicial functions in chambers Rule applied: Section 298 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: Bonser, C.J. held that a District Judge cannot ordinarily exercise judicial functions outside of open court, noting that the Civil Procedure Code does not provide for "chambers" in the manner of superior courts. While Lawrie, J. argued that certain judicial acts could be performed in chambers (including the judge's house), the majority concluded that the orders were irregular as they were made in the absence of the debtor and outside of the court's formal proceedings. Finding: The orders were irregular and invalid as they were made outside of open court without the debtor present. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the Judge lacked jurisdiction because the debtor was not present (Issue 1) is compounded by the fact that the proceedings were conducted at the Judge's private residence (Issue 2). Together, these procedural failures led the Court to conclude that the entire sequence of orders was fundamentally flawed and required quashing. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The orders of committal and discharge made by the District Judge are quashed.

Keywords: Scope of statutory toll exemption — Powers of Government Agent to issue exemption certificates — Interpretation of Toll Ordinance provisions — Exemption for public officers in public service — Validity of exemption certificate — Wrongful demand for toll — Public service vehicle usage
Summary: Facts The accused was a toll-keeper convicted under section 15 of the Toll Ordinance No. 14 of 1867 for demanding and taking toll from the servants of H. L. Daniel, the Superintendent of Minor Roads. The Superintendent had provided his horse-keepers with a certificate of exemption signed by the Government Agent, which purported to exempt the Superintendent's carriages, carts, and horses from toll when used on public service. At the time the toll was demanded, the horse-keepers were returning a carriage and two horses to Colombo. The Superintendent, who was present at Panadure, provided the funds to pay the toll after his servants reported the detention. Issues Whether the Government Agent possessed the legal authority under section 7 of the Toll Ordinance No. 14 of 1867 to issue a certificate of exemption for the carriages, carts, and horses of the Superintendent of Minor Roads. Whether the accused committed an offense under section 15 of the Toll Ordinance No. 14 of 1867 by demanding toll from the Superintendent's servants under the circumstances. Applicable Law Statutes: Toll Ordinance No. 14 of 1867, ss 7, 15 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Authority to issue exemption Rule applied: Section 7 of the Toll Ordinance No. 14 of 1867. Court's reasoning: The court examined the scope of the Government Agent's power under section 7. It found that the statute specifically enumerates the categories for which a Government Agent may issue a written direction for toll exemption, such as cattle driven to grass, agricultural instruments, seed grain, and children traveling to school. The court determined that the certificate issued by the Government Agent exceeded these statutory powers, as it purported to grant a general exemption not contemplated by the Ordinance. Finding: The Government Agent had no legal power to issue the certificate of exemption in question. Issue 2: Liability of the accused Rule applied: Section 15 of the Toll Ordinance No. 14 of 1867. Court's reasoning: The court noted that while the Superintendent of Minor Roads enjoys immunity from toll when engaged in work on a road within ten miles of a toll station, this immunity is derived directly from the Ordinance, not from an unauthorized certificate. In this instance, the servants were returning a carriage to Colombo, and the court suggested the proper procedure would have been to provide a certificate verifying the specific nature of the public service being performed. Although the court observed that the accused was ill-advised to demand the toll, it concluded that the act did not constitute a breach of the law. Finding: The conviction was not supported by the law as the accused did not commit an offense under section 15. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked because the invalidity of the certificate (Issue 1) meant that the accused was not legally bound to honor it as an exemption. Consequently, the absence of a valid legal basis for the exemption meant that the demand for toll by the accused did not satisfy the elements of the offense under section 15 (Issue 2). Conclusion The judgment of the lower court is set aside. The accused is acquitted and discharged.
